docs.text: How to Add a New Rule to Content Editorial Assistant ¶ Overview ¶ This guide provides step-by-step instructions for adding a new rule to the Content Editorial Assistant application. The rule system is designed for easy extensibility with automatic discovery, so adding a new rule requires updating several files but follows a clear pattern. What You 'll Create: * A new rule class implementing your style check * Configuration updates to specify when the rule applies * Package imports for automatic discovery * Tests to verify the rule works correctly Understanding the Rule System ¶ Rule Categories ¶ Rules are organized into three main categories, each with its own base class: Category Base Class Purpose Language & Grammar BaseLanguageRule Word usage, grammar, style issues Punctuation BasePunctuationRule Punctuation marks and symbols Structure & Format BaseStructureRule Document structure and formatting Rule Discovery ¶ The system automatically discovers rules using the RulesRegistry class, which: 1. Scans directories up to 4 levels deep in the rules/ folder 2. Finds rule files ending with _rule.py 3. Instantiates rule classes that inherit from BaseRule 4. Registers rules by their unique rule type identifier Rule Application ¶ Rules are applied contextually based on: * Block type (paragraph, heading, list item, etc.) * Rule mappings defined in rules/rule_mappings.yaml * Context information from the structural parser Step-by-Step Guide ¶ Step 1: Choose Rule Category and Location ¶ Determine which category your rule belongs to and create it in the appropriate directory: [code] rules/ ├── language_and_grammar/ # Language and grammar rules ├── punctuation/ # Punctuation rules ├── structure_and_format/ # Structure and format rules └── (root) # General rules (sentence_length, etc.) [/code] Examples: * Word choice rule → rules/language_and_grammar/word_choice_rule.py * Comma usage rule → rules/punctuation/comma_usage_rule.py * Table formatting rule → rules/structure_and_format/table_formatting_rule.py * General writing rule → rules/my_general_rule.py Step 2: Create the Rule Class ¶ Create your rule file following the naming convention <rule_name>_rule.py . For Language & Grammar Rules ¶ [code] """ My Custom Language Rule Based on [Style Guide Reference or Custom Requirement] """ from typing import List, Dict, Any from .base_language_rule import BaseLanguageRule class MyCustomLanguageRule ( BaseLanguageRule ) : """ Description of what this rule checks for. Include any relevant style guide references. """ def _get_rule_type ( self ) -> str : """Returns the unique identifier for this rule.""" return 'my_custom_language' # Must be unique across all rules def analyze ( self , text : str , sentences : List [ str ] , nlp = None , context = None ) -> List [ Dict[str, Any ] ]: """ Analyzes sentences for the specific issue this rule detects. Args: text: Full text content sentences: List of individual sentences nlp: SpaCy nlp object (optional, required for linguistic analysis) context: Block context information (optional) Returns: List of error dictionaries """ errors = [] # Return early if dependencies not available if not nlp : # If your rule requires SpaCy , return empty list when not available return errors # Analyze each sentence for i , sentence in enumerate ( sentences ) : if not sentence . strip () : continue # Use SpaCy for linguistic analysis doc = nlp ( sentence ) # Your detection logic here for token in doc : if self . _should_flag_token ( token ) : errors . append ( self . _create_error ( sentence = sentence , sentence_index = i , message = f "Issue detected: {token.text}" , suggestions =[ "Suggestion 1 for fixing the issue", "Alternative suggestion" ] , severity = 'medium' # 'low' , 'medium' , 'high' )) return errors def _should_flag_token ( self , token ) : """Helper method for detection logic.""" # Your specific detection logic return False [/code] For Punctuation Rules ¶ [code] """ My Custom Punctuation Rule Based on [Style Guide Reference] """ from typing import List, Dict, Any from .base_punctuation_rule import BasePunctuationRule class MyCustomPunctuationRule ( BasePunctuationRule ) : """ Description of punctuation issue this rule detects. """ def _get_rule_type ( self ) -> str : """Returns the unique identifier for this rule.""" return 'my_custom_punctuation' def analyze ( self , text : str , sentences : List [ str ] , nlp = None , context = None ) -> List [ Dict[str, Any ] ]: """ Analyzes sentences for punctuation issues. """ errors = [] for i , sentence in enumerate ( sentences ) : # Your punctuation detection logic if self . _has_punctuation_issue ( sentence ) : errors . append ( self . _create_error ( sentence = sentence , sentence_index = i , message = "Punctuation issue detected" , suggestions =[ "Fix suggestion" ] , severity = 'low' )) return errors def _has_punctuation_issue ( self , sentence : str ) -> bool : """Detect punctuation issues.""" # Your logic here return False [/code] For Structure & Format Rules ¶ [code] """ My Custom Structure Rule Based on [Style Guide Reference] """ from typing import List, Dict, Any from .base_structure_rule import BaseStructureRule class MyCustomStructureRule ( BaseStructureRule ) : """ Description of structural issue this rule detects. """ def _get_rule_type ( self ) -> str : """Returns the unique identifier for this rule.""" return 'my_custom_structure' def analyze ( self , text : str , sentences : List [ str ] , nlp = None , context = None ) -> List [ Dict[str, Any ] ]: """ Analyzes text for structural issues. """ errors = [] # Access block context if needed block_type = context . get ( 'block_type' ) if context else None # Your structural analysis logic if block_type == 'heading' and self . _has_heading_issue ( text ) : errors . append ( self . _create_error ( sentence = text , sentence_index = 0 , message = "Structural issue in heading" , suggestions =[ "Structure improvement suggestion" ] , severity = 'medium' )) return errors def _has_heading_issue ( self , text : str ) -> bool : """Detect heading structure issues.""" # Your logic here return False [/code] Step 3: Update Package Imports ¶ Add your rule to the appropriate package's _init_.py file to enable automatic discovery. For Language & Grammar Rules ¶ Edit rules/language_and_grammar/_init_.py : [code] """ Language and Grammar Rules Package """ # ... existing imports ... from .my_custom_language_rule import MyCustomLanguageRule __all__ = [ # ... existing rules ... 'MyCustomLanguageRule' ] [/code] For Punctuation Rules ¶ Edit rules/punctuation/_init_.py : [code] """ Punctuation Rules Package """ # ... existing imports ... from .my_custom_punctuation_rule import MyCustomPunctuationRule __all__ = [ # ... existing rules ... 'MyCustomPunctuationRule' ] [/code] For Structure & Format Rules ¶ Edit rules/structure_and_format/_init_.py : [code] """ Structure and Format Rules Package """ # ... existing imports ... from .my_custom_structure_rule import MyCustomStructureRule __all__ = [ # ... existing rules ... 'MyCustomStructureRule' ] [/code] Step 4: Configure Rule Mappings ¶ Edit rules/rule_mappings.yaml to specify which block types your rule should analyze. Understanding Block Types ¶ Common block types include: * paragraph - Regular text paragraphs * heading - Document headings * list_item - Items in lists * admonition - Warning/note blocks * code_block - Code listings * table_cell - Table content Adding Rule Mappings ¶ Add your rule identifier to the appropriate block types: [code] block_type_rules: # Apply to paragraph blocks paragraph: # ... existing rules ... - my_custom_language # Add your rule identifier here # Apply to heading blocks heading : # ... existing rules ... - my_custom_punctuation # If applicable to headings # Apply to list items list_item : # ... existing rules ... - my_custom_structure # If applicable to lists # Optional : Exclude rule from specific block types rule_exclusions : code_block : - my_custom_language # Don ' t apply language rules to code [/code] Rule Mapping Guidelines ¶ Rule Type Recommended Block Types Language Rules paragraph , list_item , admonition Punctuation Rules paragraph , heading , list_item , admonition Structure Rules Depends on what structure you're checking Step 5: Test Your Rule ¶ Create a test to verify your rule works correctly. Create Test File ¶ Create test_my_custom_rule.py : [code] """ Tests for My Custom Rule """ import pytest import spacy from rules.language_and_grammar.my_custom_language_rule import MyCustomLanguageRule class TestMyCustomLanguageRule : def setup_method ( self ) : """Setup test fixtures.""" self . rule = MyCustomLanguageRule () try : self . nlp = spacy . load ( "en_core_web_sm" ) except OSError : self . nlp = None def test_rule_type ( self ) : """Test rule type identifier.""" assert self . rule . _get_rule_type () == 'my_custom_language' def test_detects_issue ( self ) : """Test that rule detects the target issue.""" if not self . nlp : pytest . skip ( "SpaCy model not available" ) # Text that should trigger the rule text = "Text that contains the issue you're detecting" sentences = [ text ] errors = self . rule . analyze ( text , sentences , self . nlp ) assert len ( errors ) > 0 assert errors [ 0 ][ 'type' ] == 'my_custom_language' assert 'message' in errors [ 0 ] assert 'suggestions' in errors [ 0 ] def test_no_false_positives ( self ) : """Test that rule doesn't flag correct text.""" if not self . nlp : pytest . skip ( "SpaCy model not available" ) # Text that should NOT trigger the rule text = "Correct text that should pass the rule" sentences = [ text ] errors = self . rule . analyze ( text , sentences , self . nlp ) assert len ( errors ) == 0 def test_handles_empty_input ( self ) : """Test rule handles empty input gracefully.""" errors = self . rule . analyze ( "" , [] , self . nlp ) assert errors == [] def test_works_without_spacy ( self ) : """Test rule handles missing SpaCy gracefully.""" # If your rule requires SpaCy , it should return empty list when not available errors = self . rule . analyze ( "Some text" , [ "Some text" ] , None ) assert isinstance ( errors , list ) [/code] Run Tests ¶ [code] # Run your specific test python -m pytest test_my_custom_rule.py -v # Run all rule tests python -m pytest rules/ -v [/code] Step 6: Verify Integration ¶ Check Rule Discovery ¶ Test that your rule is discovered by the system: [code] # Test script to verify rule discovery from rules import get_registry registry = get_registry () discovered = registry . list_discovered_rules () print ( "Discovered rules:" ) for location , rules in discovered [ ' rules_by_location ' ]. items (): print ( f " {location}: {rules}" ) # Check if your rule is in the list rule_types = discovered [ ' all_rule_types ' ] assert ' my_custom_language ' in rule_types , "Rule not discovered!" print ( "✅ Rule successfully discovered!" ) [/code] Test Rule Application ¶ Test that your rule is applied to the correct block types: [code] # Test rule application from style_analyzer import StyleAnalyzer analyzer = StyleAnalyzer () # Test text that should trigger your rule test_text = """ This is a test paragraph that contains issues your rule should detect. # This is a heading - This is a list item """ result = analyzer . analyze ( test_text ) errors = result . get ( ' errors ' , []) # Check if your rule was applied rule_errors = [ e for e in errors if e . get ( ' type ' ) == ' my_custom_language ' ] print ( f "Found {len(rule_errors)} errors from your rule" ) [/code] Advanced Topics ¶ Context-Aware Rules ¶ Use the context parameter to make rules aware of their block type: [code] def analyze(self, text: str, sentences: List[str], nlp=None, context=None) -> List[Dict[str, Any]]: errors = [] # Get block type from context block_type = context . get ( ' block_type ' ) if context else ' paragraph ' # Apply different logic based on block type if block_type == ' heading ' : # Heading - specific analysis errors . extend ( self . _analyze_heading ( text , nlp )) elif block_type == ' list_item ' : # List item - specific analysis errors . extend ( self . _analyze_list_item ( text , nlp )) else : # Default paragraph analysis errors . extend ( self . _analyze_paragraph ( text , sentences , nlp )) return errors [/code] Performance Optimization ¶ For rules that might be expensive: [code] def analyze(self, text: str, sentences: List[str], nlp=None, context=None) -> List[Dict[str, Any]]: # Early exit for short text if len(text) < 10: return [] # Cache expensive computations if not hasattr(self, '_cached_patterns'): self._cached_patterns = self._compile_patterns() # Use efficient algorithms return self._fast_analysis(text, sentences, nlp) [/code] Custom Error Types ¶ Create specialized error information: [code] def _create_custom_error(self, sentence: str, sentence_index: int, issue_type: str, problematic_text: str) -> Dict[str, Any]: """Create error with custom fields.""" error = self._create_error( sentence=sentence, sentence_index=sentence_index, message=f"{issue_type} issue: {problematic_text}", suggestions=self._get_suggestions_for_type(issue_type), severity=self._get_severity_for_type(issue_type) ) # Add custom fields error [ ' issue_type ' ] = issue_type error [ ' problematic_text ' ] = problematic_text error [ ' rule_category ' ] = ' language ' return error [/code] Files Summary ¶ When adding a new rule, you'll typically update these files: File Purpose Required? rules/category/my_rule.py Your rule implementation ✅ Required rules/category/_init_.py Package imports ✅ Required rules/rule_mappings.yaml Block type configuration ✅ Required test_my_rule.py Unit tests 🔸 Recommended Optional Files (Advanced Cases) ¶ File Purpose When Needed rules/my_rule_config.yaml Rule-specific configuration Complex rules with settings rules/base_my_category_rule.py New category base class Creating new rule category rules/_init_.py Registry modifications Advanced registry changes Best Practices ¶ Rule Design ¶ 1. Single Responsibility : Each rule should check for one specific issue 2. Clear Naming : Use descriptive names that indicate what the rule checks 3. Robust Error Handling : Handle missing dependencies gracefully 4. Informative Messages : Provide clear, actionable error messages Error Messages ¶ Good error messages should: * Explain the issue : What style problem was detected? * Provide context : Where in the text is the problem? * Offer solutions : How can the user fix the issue? [code] # Good error message self._create_error( sentence=sentence, sentence_index=i, message=f"Passive voice without clear actor: '{problematic_phrase}'", suggestions=[ "Identify who or what is performing the action", "Rewrite in active voice: 'The system processes...' instead of 'Data is processed...'" ], severity='medium' ) # Poor error message self._create_error( sentence=sentence, sentence_index=i, message="Bad grammar", # Too vague suggestions=["Fix it"], # Not helpful severity='high' # Probably too severe ) [/code] Testing Strategy ¶ 1. Positive Tests : Verify the rule detects issues it should catch 2. Negative Tests : Ensure the rule doesn't flag correct text 3. Edge Cases : Test with empty text, single words, very long sentences 4. Dependency Tests : Test behavior when SpaCy or other dependencies are unavailable Performance Considerations ¶ 1. Early Exit : Return quickly for text that doesn't need analysis 2. Efficient Algorithms : Use appropriate data structures and algorithms 3. Cache Expensive Operations : Store compiled patterns or models 4. Limit Scope : Only analyze relevant parts of the text Troubleshooting ¶ Common Issues ¶ Rule Not Discovered ¶ Problem : Rule doesn't appear in the discovered rules list. Solutions : 1. Check file naming: Must end with _rule.py 2. Verify class inheritance: Must inherit from appropriate base class 3. Implement required methods: _get_rule_type() and analyze() 4. Check _init_.py : Ensure rule is imported 5. Verify rule type uniqueness: Rule type must be unique across all rules Rule Not Applied ¶ Problem : Rule is discovered but doesn't run on text. Solutions : 1. Check rule_mappings.yaml : Ensure rule is mapped to appropriate block types 2. Verify block type matching: Rule identifier must match exactly 3. Check exclusions: Ensure rule isn't excluded for the block type you're testing Import Errors ¶ Problem : Import errors when loading the rule. Solutions : 1. Check import paths: Use relative imports in rule files 2. Verify base class imports: Ensure base class is available 3. Check circular imports: Avoid importing from rules that import your rule SpaCy Issues ¶ Problem : Rule fails when SpaCy is required but not available. Solutions : 1. Add dependency check: Return empty list when SpaCy is not available 2. Graceful degradation: Provide simpler analysis when NLP is unavailable 3. Clear documentation: Document SpaCy requirements Example: Complete Implementation ¶ Here's a complete example implementing a rule that detects overuse of the word "very": File: rules/language_and_grammar/intensity_modifiers_rule.py ¶ [code] """ Intensity Modifiers Rule Detects overuse of weak intensity modifiers like "very", "really", "quite" Based on best practices for clear, direct writing """ from typing import List, Dict, Any, Set from .base_language_rule import BaseLanguageRule class IntensityModifiersRule ( BaseLanguageRule ) : """ Flags overuse of weak intensity modifiers that often weaken writing. Suggests stronger alternatives or removal of unnecessary intensifiers. """ def __init__ ( self ) : super (). __init__ () # Common weak intensity modifiers self . weak_intensifiers : Set [ str ] = { 'very' , 'really' , 'quite' , 'rather' , 'extremely' , 'incredibly' , 'tremendously' , 'absolutely' , 'totally' } # Suggested alternatives self . alternatives = { 'very big' : 'huge, enormous, massive' , 'very small' : 'tiny, minuscule' , 'very good' : 'excellent, outstanding' , 'very bad' : 'terrible, awful' , 'very fast' : 'rapid, swift' , 'very slow' : 'sluggish, gradual' } def _get_rule_type ( self ) -> str : """Returns the unique identifier for this rule.""" return 'intensity_modifiers' def analyze ( self , text : str , sentences : List [ str ] , nlp = None , context = None ) -> List [ Dict[str, Any ] ]: """ Analyzes sentences for overuse of weak intensity modifiers. """ errors = [] if not nlp : # Fallback to simple word matching without SpaCy return self . _analyze_without_nlp ( sentences ) for i , sentence in enumerate ( sentences ) : if not sentence . strip () : continue doc = nlp ( sentence ) for token in doc : # Check if token is a weak intensifier if ( token . text . lower () in self . weak_intensifiers and token . pos_ in [ 'ADV', 'PART' ] ) : # Adverb or particle # Get the word being modified modified_word = self . _get_modified_word ( token ) # Create error with context - aware suggestions suggestions = self . _generate_suggestions ( token . text . lower (), modified_word ) errors . append ( self . _create_error ( sentence = sentence , sentence_index = i , message = f "Weak intensifier '{token.text}' may weaken your writing" , suggestions = suggestions , severity = 'low' )) return errors def _analyze_without_nlp ( self , sentences : List [ str ] ) -> List [ Dict[str, Any ] ]: """Fallback analysis without SpaCy.""" errors = [] for i , sentence in enumerate ( sentences ) : words = sentence . lower (). split () for word in words : if word in self . weak_intensifiers : errors . append ( self . _create_error ( sentence = sentence , sentence_index = i , message = f "Consider removing or replacing '{word}'" , suggestions =[ "Remove the intensifier for more direct writing", "Use a more specific adjective instead" ] , severity = 'low' )) return errors def _get_modified_word ( self , token ) -> str : """Get the word being modified by the intensifier.""" # Look for adjacent adjectives or verbs for child in token . children : if child . pos_ in [ 'ADJ', 'VERB' ] : return child . text # Look at the next token if token . i + 1 < len ( token . doc ) : next_token = token . doc [ token.i + 1 ] if next_token . pos_ in [ 'ADJ', 'VERB' ] : return next_token . text return "" def _generate_suggestions ( self , intensifier : str , modified_word : str ) -> List [ str ] : """Generate context-aware suggestions.""" suggestions = [] # Check for specific combinations we have alternatives for combination = f "{intensifier} {modified_word.lower()}" if combination in self . alternatives : suggestions . append ( f "Consider: {self.alternatives[combination]}" ) # General suggestions suggestions . extend ( [ f"Remove '{intensifier}' for more direct writing", "Use a more specific adjective instead of an intensifier", "Consider if the intensifier adds meaningful information" ] ) return suggestions [/code] File: Update rules/language_and_grammar/_init_.py ¶ [code] """ Language and Grammar Rules Package """ # ... existing imports ... from .intensity_modifiers_rule import IntensityModifiersRule __all__ = [ # ... existing rules ... 'IntensityModifiersRule' ] [/code] File: Update rules/rule_mappings.yaml ¶ [code] block_type_rules: paragraph: # ... existing rules ... - intensity_modifiers list_item: # ... existing rules ... - intensity_modifiers admonition: # ... existing rules ... - intensity_modifiers [/code] File: test_intensity_modifiers_rule.py ¶ [code] """ Tests for Intensity Modifiers Rule """ import pytest import spacy from rules.language_and_grammar.intensity_modifiers_rule import IntensityModifiersRule class TestIntensityModifiersRule : def setup_method ( self ) : self . rule = IntensityModifiersRule () try : self . nlp = spacy . load ( "en_core_web_sm" ) except OSError : self . nlp = None def test_rule_type ( self ) : assert self . rule . _get_rule_type () == 'intensity_modifiers' def test_detects_very ( self ) : text = "This is very good software." sentences = [ text ] errors = self . rule . analyze ( text , sentences , self . nlp ) assert len ( errors ) > 0 assert any ( 'very' in error [ 'message' ] for error in errors ) def test_detects_multiple_intensifiers ( self ) : text = "This is really very incredibly good." sentences = [ text ] errors = self . rule . analyze ( text , sentences , self . nlp ) # Should detect multiple intensifiers assert len ( errors ) >= 2 def test_no_false_positives ( self ) : text = "This is excellent software with outstanding performance." sentences = [ text ] errors = self . rule . analyze ( text , sentences , self . nlp ) assert len ( errors ) == 0 def test_works_without_spacy ( self ) : text = "This is very good." sentences = [ text ] errors = self . rule . analyze ( text , sentences , None ) assert len ( errors ) > 0 assert isinstance ( errors , list ) [/code] This complete example demonstrates all the concepts covered in this guide and provides a working rule that can be integrated into the system immediately. Conclusion ¶ Adding a new rule to Content Editorial Assistant involves creating a rule class, updating configuration files, and testing the integration. The modular architecture makes this process straightforward while providing powerful capabilities for sophisticated style analysis. Key Takeaways: 1. Follow the patterns : Use existing rules as templates 2. Update all required files : Implementation, imports, configuration 3. Test thoroughly : Verify discovery, application, and correctness 4. Handle dependencies gracefully : Provide fallbacks when tools unavailable 5. Write clear error messages : Help users understand and fix issues The automatic discovery system means that once you've created and configured your rule correctly, it will be immediately available throughout the application without requiring changes to the core analysis engine. Last updated 2025-09-22 13:41:04 +0530

The rule system is designed for easy extensibility with automatic discovery, so adding a new rule requires updating several files but follows a clear pattern. What You 'll Create: * A new rule class implementing your style check * Configuration updates to specify when the rule applies * Package imports for automatic discovery * Tests to verify the rule works correctly

Instantiates rule classes that inherit from BaseRule 4. Registers rules by their unique rule type identifier

docs.text: [code] """ My Custom Language Rule Based on [Style Guide Reference or Custom Requirement] """ from typing import List, Dict, Any from .base_language_rule import BaseLanguageRule class MyCustomLanguageRule ( BaseLanguageRule ) : """ Description of what this rule checks for. Include any relevant style guide references. """ def _get_rule_type ( self ) -> str : """Returns the unique identifier for this rule.""" return 'my_custom_language' # Must be unique across all rules def analyze ( self , text : str , sentences : List [ str ] , nlp = None , context = None ) -> List [ Dict[str, Any ] ]: """ Analyzes sentences for the specific issue this rule detects. Args: text: Full text content sentences: List of individual sentences nlp: SpaCy nlp object (optional, required for linguistic analysis) context: Block context information (optional) Returns: List of error dictionaries """ errors = [] # Return early if dependencies not available if not nlp : # If your rule requires SpaCy , return empty list when not available return errors # Analyze each sentence for i , sentence in enumerate ( sentences ) : if not sentence . strip () : continue # Use SpaCy for linguistic analysis doc = nlp ( sentence ) # Your detection logic here for token in doc : if self . _should_flag_token ( token ) : errors . append ( self . _create_error ( sentence = sentence , sentence_index = i , message = f "Issue detected: {token.text}" , suggestions =[ "Suggestion 1 for fixing the issue", "Alternative suggestion" ] , severity = 'medium' # 'low' , 'medium' , 'high' )) return errors def _should_flag_token ( self , token ) : """Helper method for detection logic.""" # Your specific detection logic return False [/code]
docs.title: For Language & Grammar Rules

docs.text: [code] """ My Custom Punctuation Rule Based on [Style Guide Reference] """ from typing import List, Dict, Any from .base_punctuation_rule import BasePunctuationRule class MyCustomPunctuationRule ( BasePunctuationRule ) : """ Description of punctuation issue this rule detects. """ def _get_rule_type ( self ) -> str : """Returns the unique identifier for this rule.""" return 'my_custom_punctuation' def analyze ( self , text : str , sentences : List [ str ] , nlp = None , context = None ) -> List [ Dict[str, Any ] ]: """ Analyzes sentences for punctuation issues. """ errors = [] for i , sentence in enumerate ( sentences ) : # Your punctuation detection logic if self . _has_punctuation_issue ( sentence ) : errors . append ( self . _create_error ( sentence = sentence , sentence_index = i , message = "Punctuation issue detected" , suggestions =[ "Fix suggestion" ] , severity = 'low' )) return errors def _has_punctuation_issue ( self , sentence : str ) -> bool : """Detect punctuation issues.""" # Your logic here return False [/code]
docs.title: For Punctuation Rules

docs.text: [code] """ My Custom Structure Rule Based on [Style Guide Reference] """ from typing import List, Dict, Any from .base_structure_rule import BaseStructureRule class MyCustomStructureRule ( BaseStructureRule ) : """ Description of structural issue this rule detects. """ def _get_rule_type ( self ) -> str : """Returns the unique identifier for this rule.""" return 'my_custom_structure' def analyze ( self , text : str , sentences : List [ str ] , nlp = None , context = None ) -> List [ Dict[str, Any ] ]: """ Analyzes text for structural issues. """ errors = [] # Access block context if needed block_type = context . get ( 'block_type' ) if context else None # Your structural analysis logic if block_type == 'heading' and self . _has_heading_issue ( text ) : errors . append ( self . _create_error ( sentence = text , sentence_index = 0 , message = "Structural issue in heading" , suggestions =[ "Structure improvement suggestion" ] , severity = 'medium' )) return errors def _has_heading_issue ( self , text : str ) -> bool : """Detect heading structure issues.""" # Your logic here return False [/code]
docs.title: For Structure & Format Rules

docs.text: Add your rule to the appropriate package's _init_.py file to enable automatic discovery.
docs.title: Step 3: Update Package Imports

docs.text: Edit rules/language_and_grammar/_init_.py : [code] """ Language and Grammar Rules Package """ # ... existing imports ... from .my_custom_language_rule import MyCustomLanguageRule __all__ = [ # ... existing rules ... 'MyCustomLanguageRule' ] [/code]
docs.title: For Language & Grammar Rules

docs.text: Edit rules/punctuation/_init_.py : [code] """ Punctuation Rules Package """ # ... existing imports ... from .my_custom_punctuation_rule import MyCustomPunctuationRule __all__ = [ # ... existing rules ... 'MyCustomPunctuationRule' ] [/code]
docs.title: For Punctuation Rules

docs.text: Edit rules/structure_and_format/_init_.py : [code] """ Structure and Format Rules Package """ # ... existing imports ... from .my_custom_structure_rule import MyCustomStructureRule __all__ = [ # ... existing rules ... 'MyCustomStructureRule' ] [/code]
docs.title: For Structure & Format Rules

docs.text: Edit rules/rule_mappings.yaml to specify which block types your rule should analyze.
docs.title: Step 4: Configure Rule Mappings

docs.text: Common block types include: * paragraph - Regular text paragraphs * heading - Document headings * list_item - Items in lists * admonition - Warning/note blocks * code_block - Code listings * table_cell - Table content
docs.title: Understanding Block Types

docs.text: Add your rule identifier to the appropriate block types: [code] block_type_rules: # Apply to paragraph blocks paragraph: # ... existing rules ... - my_custom_language # Add your rule identifier here # Apply to heading blocks heading : # ... existing rules ... - my_custom_punctuation # If applicable to headings # Apply to list items list_item : # ... existing rules ... - my_custom_structure # If applicable to lists # Optional : Exclude rule from specific block types rule_exclusions : code_block : - my_custom_language # Don ' t apply language rules to code [/code]
docs.title: Adding Rule Mappings

docs.text: Rule Type Recommended Block Types Language Rules paragraph , list_item , admonition Punctuation Rules paragraph , heading , list_item , admonition Structure Rules Depends on what structure you're checking
docs.title: Rule Mapping Guidelines

docs.text: Create a test to verify your rule works correctly.
docs.title: Step 5: Test Your Rule

docs.text: Create test_my_custom_rule.py : [code] """ Tests for My Custom Rule """ import pytest import spacy from rules.language_and_grammar.my_custom_language_rule import MyCustomLanguageRule class TestMyCustomLanguageRule : def setup_method ( self ) : """Setup test fixtures.""" self . rule = MyCustomLanguageRule () try : self . nlp = spacy . load ( "en_core_web_sm" ) except OSError : self . nlp = None def test_rule_type ( self ) : """Test rule type identifier.""" assert self . rule . _get_rule_type () == 'my_custom_language' def test_detects_issue ( self ) : """Test that rule detects the target issue.""" if not self . nlp : pytest . skip ( "SpaCy model not available" ) # Text that should trigger the rule text = "Text that contains the issue you're detecting" sentences = [ text ] errors = self . rule . analyze ( text , sentences , self . nlp ) assert len ( errors ) > 0 assert errors [ 0 ][ 'type' ] == 'my_custom_language' assert 'message' in errors [ 0 ] assert 'suggestions' in errors [ 0 ] def test_no_false_positives ( self ) : """Test that rule doesn't flag correct text.""" if not self . nlp : pytest . skip ( "SpaCy model not available" ) # Text that should NOT trigger the rule text = "Correct text that should pass the rule" sentences = [ text ] errors = self . rule . analyze ( text , sentences , self . nlp ) assert len ( errors ) == 0 def test_handles_empty_input ( self ) : """Test rule handles empty input gracefully.""" errors = self . rule . analyze ( "" , [] , self . nlp ) assert errors == [] def test_works_without_spacy ( self ) : """Test rule handles missing SpaCy gracefully.""" # If your rule requires SpaCy , it should return empty list when not available errors = self . rule . analyze ( "Some text" , [ "Some text" ] , None ) assert isinstance ( errors , list ) [/code]
docs.title: Create Test File

docs.text: [code] # Run your specific test python -m pytest test_my_custom_rule.py -v # Run all rule tests python -m pytest rules/ -v [/code]
docs.title: Run Tests

docs.text: Test that your rule is discovered by the system: [code] # Test script to verify rule discovery from rules import get_registry registry = get_registry () discovered = registry . list_discovered_rules () print ( "Discovered rules:" ) for location , rules in discovered [ ' rules_by_location ' ]. items (): print ( f " {location}: {rules}" ) # Check if your rule is in the list rule_types = discovered [ ' all_rule_types ' ] assert ' my_custom_language ' in rule_types , "Rule not discovered!" print ( "✅ Rule successfully discovered!" ) [/code]
docs.title: Check Rule Discovery

docs.text: Test that your rule is applied to the correct block types: [code] # Test rule application from style_analyzer import StyleAnalyzer analyzer = StyleAnalyzer () # Test text that should trigger your rule test_text = """ This is a test paragraph that contains issues your rule should detect. # This is a heading - This is a list item """ result = analyzer . analyze ( test_text ) errors = result . get ( ' errors ' , []) # Check if your rule was applied rule_errors = [ e for e in errors if e . get ( ' type ' ) == ' my_custom_language ' ] print ( f "Found {len(rule_errors)} errors from your rule" ) [/code]
docs.title: Test Rule Application

docs.text: Use the context parameter to make rules aware of their block type: [code] def analyze(self, text: str, sentences: List[str], nlp=None, context=None) -> List[Dict[str, Any]]: errors = [] # Get block type from context block_type = context . get ( ' block_type ' ) if context else ' paragraph ' # Apply different logic based on block type if block_type == ' heading ' : # Heading - specific analysis errors . extend ( self . _analyze_heading ( text , nlp )) elif block_type == ' list_item ' : # List item - specific analysis errors . extend ( self . _analyze_list_item ( text , nlp )) else : # Default paragraph analysis errors . extend ( self . _analyze_paragraph ( text , sentences , nlp )) return errors [/code]
docs.title: Context-Aware Rules

docs.text: For rules that might be expensive: [code] def analyze(self, text: str, sentences: List[str], nlp=None, context=None) -> List[Dict[str, Any]]: # Early exit for short text if len(text) < 10: return [] # Cache expensive computations if not hasattr(self, '_cached_patterns'): self._cached_patterns = self._compile_patterns() # Use efficient algorithms return self._fast_analysis(text, sentences, nlp) [/code]
docs.title: Performance Optimization

docs.text: Create specialized error information: [code] def _create_custom_error(self, sentence: str, sentence_index: int, issue_type: str, problematic_text: str) -> Dict[str, Any]: """Create error with custom fields.""" error = self._create_error( sentence=sentence, sentence_index=sentence_index, message=f"{issue_type} issue: {problematic_text}", suggestions=self._get_suggestions_for_type(issue_type), severity=self._get_severity_for_type(issue_type) ) # Add custom fields error [ ' issue_type ' ] = issue_type error [ ' problematic_text ' ] = problematic_text error [ ' rule_category ' ] = ' language ' return error [/code]
docs.title: Custom Error Types

docs.text: When adding a new rule, you'll typically update these files: File Purpose Required? rules/category/my_rule.py Your rule implementation ✅ Required rules/category/_init_.py Package imports ✅ Required rules/rule_mappings.yaml Block type configuration ✅ Required test_my_rule.py Unit tests 🔸 Recommended
docs.title: Files Summary

docs.text: File Purpose When Needed rules/my_rule_config.yaml Rule-specific configuration Complex rules with settings rules/base_my_category_rule.py New category base class Creating new rule category rules/_init_.py Registry modifications Advanced registry changes
docs.title: Optional Files (Advanced Cases)

docs.text: 1. Single Responsibility : Each rule should check for one specific issue 2. Clear Naming : Use descriptive names that indicate what the rule checks 3. Robust Error Handling : Handle missing dependencies gracefully 4. Informative Messages : Provide clear, actionable error messages
docs.title: Rule Design

docs.text: Good error messages should: * Explain the issue : What style problem was detected? * Provide context : Where in the text is the problem? * Offer solutions : How can the user fix the issue? [code] # Good error message self._create_error( sentence=sentence, sentence_index=i, message=f"Passive voice without clear actor: '{problematic_phrase}'", suggestions=[ "Identify who or what is performing the action", "Rewrite in active voice: 'The system processes...' instead of 'Data is processed...'" ], severity='medium' ) # Poor error message self._create_error( sentence=sentence, sentence_index=i, message="Bad grammar", # Too vague suggestions=["Fix it"], # Not helpful severity='high' # Probably too severe ) [/code]
docs.title: Error Messages

docs.text: 1. Positive Tests : Verify the rule detects issues it should catch 2. Negative Tests : Ensure the rule doesn't flag correct text 3. Edge Cases : Test with empty text, single words, very long sentences 4. Dependency Tests : Test behavior when SpaCy or other dependencies are unavailable
docs.title: Testing Strategy

docs.text: 1. Early Exit : Return quickly for text that doesn't need analysis 2. Efficient Algorithms : Use appropriate data structures and algorithms 3. Cache Expensive Operations : Store compiled patterns or models 4. Limit Scope : Only analyze relevant parts of the text
docs.title: Performance Considerations

docs.text: Problem : Rule doesn't appear in the discovered rules list. Solutions : 1. Check file naming: Must end with _rule.py 2. Verify class inheritance: Must inherit from appropriate base class 3. Implement required methods: _get_rule_type() and analyze() 4. Check _init_.py : Ensure rule is imported 5. Verify rule type uniqueness: Rule type must be unique across all rules
docs.title: Rule Not Discovered

docs.text: Problem : Rule is discovered but doesn't run on text. Solutions : 1. Check rule_mappings.yaml : Ensure rule is mapped to appropriate block types 2. Verify block type matching: Rule identifier must match exactly 3. Check exclusions: Ensure rule isn't excluded for the block type you're testing
docs.title: Rule Not Applied

docs.text: Problem : Import errors when loading the rule. Solutions : 1. Check import paths: Use relative imports in rule files 2. Verify base class imports: Ensure base class is available 3. Check circular imports: Avoid importing from rules that import your rule
docs.title: Import Errors

docs.text: Problem : Rule fails when SpaCy is required but not available. Solutions : 1. Add dependency check: Return empty list when SpaCy is not available 2. Graceful degradation: Provide simpler analysis when NLP is unavailable 3. Clear documentation: Document SpaCy requirements
docs.title: SpaCy Issues

docs.text: Here's a complete example implementing a rule that detects overuse of the word "very":
docs.title: Example: Complete Implementation

docs.text: [code] """ Intensity Modifiers Rule Detects overuse of weak intensity modifiers like "very", "really", "quite" Based on best practices for clear, direct writing """ from typing import List, Dict, Any, Set from .base_language_rule import BaseLanguageRule class IntensityModifiersRule ( BaseLanguageRule ) : """ Flags overuse of weak intensity modifiers that often weaken writing. Suggests stronger alternatives or removal of unnecessary intensifiers. """ def __init__ ( self ) : super (). __init__ () # Common weak intensity modifiers self . weak_intensifiers : Set [ str ] = { 'very' , 'really' , 'quite' , 'rather' , 'extremely' , 'incredibly' , 'tremendously' , 'absolutely' , 'totally' } # Suggested alternatives self . alternatives = { 'very big' : 'huge, enormous, massive' , 'very small' : 'tiny, minuscule' , 'very good' : 'excellent, outstanding' , 'very bad' : 'terrible, awful' , 'very fast' : 'rapid, swift' , 'very slow' : 'sluggish, gradual' } def _get_rule_type ( self ) -> str : """Returns the unique identifier for this rule.""" return 'intensity_modifiers' def analyze ( self , text : str , sentences : List [ str ] , nlp = None , context = None ) -> List [ Dict[str, Any ] ]: """ Analyzes sentences for overuse of weak intensity modifiers. """ errors = [] if not nlp : # Fallback to simple word matching without SpaCy return self . _analyze_without_nlp ( sentences ) for i , sentence in enumerate ( sentences ) : if not sentence . strip () : continue doc = nlp ( sentence ) for token in doc : # Check if token is a weak intensifier if ( token . text . lower () in self . weak_intensifiers and token . pos_ in [ 'ADV', 'PART' ] ) : # Adverb or particle # Get the word being modified modified_word = self . _get_modified_word ( token ) # Create error with context - aware suggestions suggestions = self . _generate_suggestions ( token . text . lower (), modified_word ) errors . append ( self . _create_error ( sentence = sentence , sentence_index = i , message = f "Weak intensifier '{token.text}' may weaken your writing" , suggestions = suggestions , severity = 'low' )) return errors def _analyze_without_nlp ( self , sentences : List [ str ] ) -> List [ Dict[str, Any ] ]: """Fallback analysis without SpaCy.""" errors = [] for i , sentence in enumerate ( sentences ) : words = sentence . lower (). split () for word in words : if word in self . weak_intensifiers : errors . append ( self . _create_error ( sentence = sentence , sentence_index = i , message = f "Consider removing or replacing '{word}'" , suggestions =[ "Remove the intensifier for more direct writing", "Use a more specific adjective instead" ] , severity = 'low' )) return errors def _get_modified_word ( self , token ) -> str : """Get the word being modified by the intensifier.""" # Look for adjacent adjectives or verbs for child in token . children : if child . pos_ in [ 'ADJ', 'VERB' ] : return child . text # Look at the next token if token . i + 1 < len ( token . doc ) : next_token = token . doc [ token.i + 1 ] if next_token . pos_ in [ 'ADJ', 'VERB' ] : return next_token . text return "" def _generate_suggestions ( self , intensifier : str , modified_word : str ) -> List [ str ] : """Generate context-aware suggestions.""" suggestions = [] # Check for specific combinations we have alternatives for combination = f "{intensifier} {modified_word.lower()}" if combination in self . alternatives : suggestions . append ( f "Consider: {self.alternatives[combination]}" ) # General suggestions suggestions . extend ( [ f"Remove '{intensifier}' for more direct writing", "Use a more specific adjective instead of an intensifier", "Consider if the intensifier adds meaningful information" ] ) return suggestions [/code]
docs.title: File: rules/language_and_grammar/intensity_modifiers_rule.py

docs.text: [code] """ Language and Grammar Rules Package """ # ... existing imports ... from .intensity_modifiers_rule import IntensityModifiersRule __all__ = [ # ... existing rules ... 'IntensityModifiersRule' ] [/code]
docs.title: File: Update rules/language_and_grammar/_init_.py

docs.text: [code] block_type_rules: paragraph: # ... existing rules ... - intensity_modifiers list_item: # ... existing rules ... - intensity_modifiers admonition: # ... existing rules ... - intensity_modifiers [/code]
docs.title: File: Update rules/rule_mappings.yaml

docs.text: [code] """ Tests for Intensity Modifiers Rule """ import pytest import spacy from rules.language_and_grammar.intensity_modifiers_rule import IntensityModifiersRule class TestIntensityModifiersRule : def setup_method ( self ) : self . rule = IntensityModifiersRule () try : self . nlp = spacy . load ( "en_core_web_sm" ) except OSError : self . nlp = None def test_rule_type ( self ) : assert self . rule . _get_rule_type () == 'intensity_modifiers' def test_detects_very ( self ) : text = "This is very good software." sentences = [ text ] errors = self . rule . analyze ( text , sentences , self . nlp ) assert len ( errors ) > 0 assert any ( 'very' in error [ 'message' ] for error in errors ) def test_detects_multiple_intensifiers ( self ) : text = "This is really very incredibly good." sentences = [ text ] errors = self . rule . analyze ( text , sentences , self . nlp ) # Should detect multiple intensifiers assert len ( errors ) >= 2 def test_no_false_positives ( self ) : text = "This is excellent software with outstanding performance." sentences = [ text ] errors = self . rule . analyze ( text , sentences , self . nlp ) assert len ( errors ) == 0 def test_works_without_spacy ( self ) : text = "This is very good." sentences = [ text ] errors = self . rule . analyze ( text , sentences , None ) assert len ( errors ) > 0 assert isinstance ( errors , list ) [/code] This complete example demonstrates all the concepts covered in this guide and provides a working rule that can be integrated into the system immediately.
docs.title: File: test_intensity_modifiers_rule.py

docs.text: Adding a new rule to Content Editorial Assistant involves creating a rule class, updating configuration files, and testing the integration. The modular architecture makes this process straightforward while providing powerful capabilities for sophisticated style analysis. Key Takeaways: 1. Follow the patterns : Use existing rules as templates 2. Update all required files : Implementation, imports, configuration 3. Test thoroughly : Verify discovery, application, and correctness 4. Handle dependencies gracefully : Provide fallbacks when tools unavailable 5. Write clear error messages : Help users understand and fix issues The automatic discovery system means that once you've created and configured your rule correctly, it will be immediately available throughout the application without requiring changes to the core analysis engine. Last updated 2025-09-22 13:41:04 +0530
docs.title: Conclusion

docs.text: LLM-Based Style Rules Implementation Guide ¶ Table of Contents * Overview * Files Deleted (Moved to LLM) * Language and Grammar Rules (8 files, ~9,211 lines) * Ambiguity Module (entire directory, ~4,250 lines) * Dependency Analysis * 1. rules/language_and_grammar/ init .py * 2. rules/rule_mappings.yaml * 3. rules/ambiguity_rule.py * 4. Config Files to Delete * 5. Test Files (Archive or Delete) * 6. Validation Module Reference * 7. Documentation to Update * Decision Framework * When to Use Coded Rules (Deterministic) * When to Use LLM Rules (Judgment) * Rule Classification * Language and Grammar Rules * LLM Rule Configuration * Rule Definition Format * Python Implementation * LLM Style Checker * Integration with Existing System * Hybrid Rule Runner * Token Usage and Cost * Estimated Token Usage * Cost Estimates (Gemini 1.5 Flash) * Optimization Strategies * Adding New LLM Rules * Migration Completed * Fallback Strategy * Summary * Rule Distribution * What We Deleted * Benefits * Costs * When LLM is Unavailable Overview ¶ This guide explains the hybrid architecture for style checking: deterministic rules (coded) for simple pattern matching, and LLM-based rules for judgment-intensive checks. __ | This approach reduces maintenance burden by moving complex, context-dependent rules to LLM evaluation while keeping simple word-list rules as fast, coded checks. Key Decision : We DELETE the old coded rules for judgment-intensive checks. No parallel running, no backup. One system to maintain. Location : All LLM rules are in rules/language_and_grammar/ : * llm_style_checker.py - The Gemini-powered style checker * config/llm_style_rules.yaml - Rule definitions with IBM Style Guide references ---|--- Files Deleted (Moved to LLM) ¶ The following files have been deleted and replaced by LLM-based rules: Language and Grammar Rules (8 files, ~9,211 lines) ¶ File Lines Replaced By rules/language_and_grammar/verbs_rule.py 2,470 passive_voice , verb_tense LLM rules rules/language_and_grammar/passive_voice_analyzer.py 1,347 passive_voice LLM rule rules/language_and_grammar/articles_rule.py 1,239 articles LLM rule rules/language_and_grammar/pronouns_rule.py 584 pronouns LLM rule rules/language_and_grammar/anthropomorphism_rule.py 482 anthropomorphism LLM rule rules/language_and_grammar/conjunctions_rule.py 1,439 conjunctions LLM rule rules/language_and_grammar/prepositions_rule.py 1,204 prepositions LLM rule rules/language_and_grammar/adverbs_only_rule.py 446 adverbs_only LLM rule Ambiguity Module (entire directory, ~4,250 lines) ¶ File Lines Replaced By ambiguity/detectors/pronoun_ambiguity_detector.py 1,937 pronouns LLM rule ambiguity/detectors/unsupported_claims_detector.py 790 unsupported_claims LLM rule ambiguity/detectors/fabrication_risk_detector.py 1,023 unsupported_claims LLM rule ambiguity/detectors/missing_actor_detector.py ~500 passive_voice LLM rule ambiguity/base_ambiguity_rule.py 183 (deleted) ambiguity/types.py 296 (deleted) ambiguity/_init_.py 24 (deleted) ambiguity/config/* ~50 (deleted) Total lines deleted: ~13,461 Total lines added: ~300 (YAML config) Dependency Analysis ¶ Before deleting files, the following dependencies must be updated: 1. rules/language_and_grammar/ init .py ¶ Remove imports for deleted rules: [code] # DELETE THESE IMPORTS: from .adverbs_only_rule import AdverbsOnlyRule from .anthropomorphism_rule import AnthropomorphismRule from .articles_rule import ArticlesRule from .conjunctions_rule import ConjunctionsRule from .prepositions_rule import PrepositionsRule from .pronouns_rule import PronounsRule from .verbs_rule import VerbsRule # DELETE FROM __all__: 'AdverbsOnlyRule', 'AnthropomorphismRule', 'ArticlesRule', 'ConjunctionsRule', 'PrepositionsRule', 'PronounsRule', 'VerbsRule' [/code] 2. rules/rule_mappings.yaml ¶ Remove or comment out these rules from all block types: * verbs * pronouns * anthropomorphism * articles * prepositions * adverbs_only * conjunctions * ambiguity __ These will be handled by LLM rules, not coded rules. 3. rules/ambiguity_rule.py ¶ Delete this file - it's the wrapper that imports from ambiguity/ module. 4. Config Files to Delete ¶ * rules/language_and_grammar/config/verbs_config.yaml * rules/language_and_grammar/config/verbs_corrections.yaml * rules/language_and_grammar/config/articles_config.yaml * rules/language_and_grammar/config/articles_phonetics.yaml * rules/language_and_grammar/config/anthropomorphism_config.yaml * rules/language_and_grammar/config/anthropomorphism_entities.yaml * rules/language_and_grammar/config/conjunctions_patterns.yaml * rules/language_and_grammar/config/adverbs_only_config.yaml * rules/language_and_grammar/config/prepositions_patterns.yaml (if exists) * ambiguity/config/ambiguity_types.yaml 5. Test Files (Archive or Delete) ¶ These test files will no longer work after deletion: Test File Action tests/unit/test_verbs_rule_*.py (4 files) Archive tests/guards/test_verbs_*.py (2 files) Archive tests/unit/test_articles_rule*.py (3 files) Archive tests/unit/test_conjunctions_rule_*.py (5 files) Archive tests/test_prepositions_rule_refinements.py Archive tests/guards/test_passive_voice_*.py Archive tests/unit/test_pronoun_ambiguity_*.py Archive tests/guards/test_pronoun_*.py (2 files) Archive tests/unit/test_ambiguity_detection.py Archive tests/test_fabrication_risk_refinements.py Archive 6. Validation Module Reference ¶ File: validation/multi_pass/pass_validators/morphological_validator.py Check and update any direct imports of ambiguity module. 7. Documentation to Update ¶ * docs/modules/how-to/pages/how-to-add-new-ambiguity-detector.adoc - Archive or redirect * docs/modules/architecture/pages/architecture.adoc - Update architecture diagram Decision Framework ¶ When to Use Coded Rules (Deterministic) ¶ Criteria Example Why Deterministic Finite word list "colour" → "color" Simple lookup, no context needed Binary pattern "URL's" → "URLs" Regex can handle it State tracking "Define abbreviation on first use" Stateful but deterministic Format checking "I.B.M." → "IBM" Pattern matching When to Use LLM Rules (Judgment) ¶ Criteria Example Why LLM Needed Context changes meaning "The file is opened" - passive OK in some contexts Release notes vs procedures differ Semantic understanding "it" pronoun - what does it refer to? Needs to understand antecedent Style judgment Starting sentence with "And" Sometimes acceptable for emphasis Multiple valid options "a" vs "the" article choice Depends on whether item was introduced Rule Classification ¶ Language and Grammar Rules ¶ Rule Lines Type Rationale spelling_rule.py 718 ✅ CODED Word list: "colour"→"color" inclusive_language_rule.py 810 ✅ CODED Word list: "blacklist"→"blocklist" abbreviations_rule.py 966 ✅ CODED Latin abbrev list + first-use tracking terminology_rule.py 769 ✅ CODED IBM Terminology database lookup prefixes_rule.py 1048 ✅ CODED Word list: "pre-install"→"preinstall" plurals_rule.py 1514 ✅ CODED Pattern: apostrophe in plurals possessives_rule.py 710 ✅ CODED Apostrophe rules verbs_rule.py 2470 🤖 LLM Passive voice, tense - context dependent passive_voice_analyzer.py 1347 🤖 LLM When is passive acceptable? articles_rule.py 1239 🤖 LLM "a" vs "the" - context dependent pronouns_rule.py 584 🤖 LLM Ambiguous pronoun references anthropomorphism_rule.py 482 🤖 LLM "system knows" vs "AI learns" conjunctions_rule.py 1439 🤖 LLM Starting with "And/But" - style judgment prepositions_rule.py 1204 🤖 LLM Ending with preposition - often fine adverbs_only_rule.py 446 🤖 LLM "only" placement ambiguity LLM Rule Configuration ¶ Rule Definition Format ¶ Create llm_style_rules.yaml : [code] # LLM-Based Style Rules Configuration # These rules require contextual judgment and are evaluated by Gemini version : "1.0" model : "gemini-1.5-flash" rules : # ============================================================ # VERBS AND VOICE # ============================================================ passive_voice : id : "passive_voice" category : "language_and_grammar" enabled : true severity : "medium" description : | Identifies inappropriate passive voice constructions . Active voice is preferred in technical documentation . ibm_style_reference : pages : [ 113 , 114 , 115 , 116 ] section : "Language and grammar > Verbs" summary : | Use active voice in most cases . Passive voice is acceptable when : - The actor is unknown or unimportant - In release notes describing past events - When emphasizing the action over the actor check_prompt : | Is this sentence using passive voice inappropriately ? Consider : Is this a procedure ( should be active ) or a description where passive might be acceptable ? examples : violations : - text : "The file should be opened by the user." context : "procedure step" fix : "Open the file." - text : "The configuration is recommended to be updated." context : "procedure" fix : "Update the configuration." acceptable : - text : "The bug was fixed in version 2.0." context : "release notes" reason : "Passive acceptable in release notes" - text : "The data is stored in the database." context : "description" reason : "Actor (system) is obvious" # ============================================================ # ARTICLES # ============================================================ articles : id : "articles" category : "language_and_grammar" enabled : true severity : "low" description : | Checks for missing , incorrect , or inconsistent article usage ( a / an / the ) . ibm_style_reference : pages : [ 91 , 92 ] section : "Language and grammar > Articles" summary : | Use "a" before consonant sounds , "an" before vowel sounds . This applies to pronunciation , not spelling : - "an SQL query" ( ess - queue - el ) - "a URL" ( you - are - el ) - "an hour" ( silent h ) check_prompt : | Is the article usage correct in this sentence ? Check for : 1. Missing articles where needed 2. Wrong article ( a / an based on sound ) 3. Unnecessary articles examples : violations : - text : "Deploy pod to the cluster." fix : "Deploy the pod to the cluster." reason : "Missing article before 'pod'" - text : "This is a SQL query." fix : "This is an SQL query." reason : "SQL is pronounced 'ess-queue-el' (vowel sound)" acceptable : - text : "Kubernetes uses pods for deployment." reason : "Generic/plural usage, no article needed" # ============================================================ # PRONOUNS # ============================================================ pronouns : id : "pronouns" category : "language_and_grammar" enabled : true severity : "medium" description : | Identifies ambiguous pronoun references where the antecedent is unclear . ibm_style_reference : pages : [ 109 , 110 , 111 ] section : "Language and grammar > Pronouns" summary : | Ensure pronouns have clear antecedents . "This" and "that" at the start of sentences should be followed by a noun . Avoid ambiguous pronoun references . check_prompt : | Does this pronoun have a clear antecedent ? Flag if : 1. "It" could refer to multiple things 2. "This" or "That" starts a sentence without a noun 3. The referent is ambiguous examples : violations : - text : "Configure the server and the database. It should be restarted." fix : "Configure the server and the database. The server should be restarted." reason : "'It' could refer to server or database" - text : "This is important for security." fix : "This configuration is important for security." reason : "'This' needs a noun" acceptable : - text : "Create a pod. It runs your application." reason : "Clear single antecedent (pod)" # ============================================================ # ANTHROPOMORPHISM # ============================================================ anthropomorphism : id : "anthropomorphism" category : "language_and_grammar" enabled : true severity : "low" description : | Flags inappropriate attribution of human characteristics to software . ibm_style_reference : pages : [ 90 ] section : "Language and grammar > Anthropomorphism" summary : | Do not attribute human characteristics to software or hardware . Write "the system processes" not "the system thinks" . Avoid : knows , thinks , wants , sees , remembers , decides . check_prompt : | Does this sentence inappropriately attribute human qualities to software ? Note : AI / ML systems CAN "learn" and "predict" - these are technical terms . examples : violations : - text : "The application knows your preferences." fix : "The application stores your preferences." - text : "The system wants you to restart." fix : "The system requires a restart." acceptable : - text : "The AI model learns from the training data." reason : "'Learns' is a valid ML term" - text : "The recommendation engine predicts user behavior." reason : "'Predicts' is a valid ML term" # ============================================================ # CONJUNCTIONS # ============================================================ conjunctions : id : "conjunctions" category : "language_and_grammar" enabled : true severity : "low" description : | Checks for sentences starting with conjunctions and parallel structure issues . ibm_style_reference : pages : [ 101 , 102 ] section : "Language and grammar > Conjunctions" summary : | It 's acceptable to start sentences with "And" or "But" for emphasis, but use sparingly . Ensure parallel structure with coordinating conjunctions . check_prompt : | Is the conjunction usage appropriate here ? 1. Starting with And / But - is it for emphasis or just lazy writing ? 2. Is parallel structure maintained ? examples : violations : - text : "And then you click the button." fix : "Then click the button." reason : "Unnecessary conjunction in procedure" acceptable : - text : "But this approach has limitations." reason : "Intentional emphasis after contrasting point" # ============================================================ # PREPOSITIONS # ============================================================ prepositions : id : "prepositions" category : "language_and_grammar" enabled : true severity : "low" description : | Checks for awkward prepositional phrases and ending sentences with prepositions . ibm_style_reference : pages : [ 108 , 109 ] section : "Language and grammar > Prepositions" summary : | It 's acceptable to end sentences with prepositions when natural. Avoid excessive prepositional phrases . check_prompt : | Is the preposition usage natural and clear ? Ending with a preposition is usually fine in technical docs . Flag only if it sounds awkward or unclear . examples : acceptable : - text : "Select the file you want to work on." reason : "Natural phrasing, ending with preposition is fine" - text : "This is the server the app connects to." reason : "Clear and natural" # ============================================================ # ADVERBS - ONLY # ============================================================ adverbs_only : id : "adverbs_only" category : "language_and_grammar" enabled : true severity : "low" description : | Checks for ambiguous placement of the word "only" . ibm_style_reference : pages : [ 89 ] section : "Language and grammar > Adverbs - only" summary : | Place "only" immediately before the word or phrase it modifies . Incorrect placement can change meaning . check_prompt : | Is "only" placed correctly to convey the intended meaning ? "You can only edit" vs "You can edit only" have different meanings . examples : violations : - text : "You can only edit files in this directory." ambiguity : "Does 'only' modify 'edit' or 'this directory'?" options : - "You can edit only files in this directory." - "You can edit files only in this directory." acceptable : - text : "You only need to restart the service." reason : "Clear meaning - restart is the only required action" # ============================================================ # UNSUPPORTED CLAIMS (from ambiguity module) # ============================================================ unsupported_claims : id : "unsupported_claims" category : "legal_information" enabled : true severity : "high" description : | Detects unsupported claims , promises , and absolute statements that could be legally problematic or misleading . ibm_style_reference : pages : [ 279 , 280 ] section : "Legal information > Claims and recommendations" summary : | Do not make unsubstantiated claims . Avoid absolute terms like "always" and "never" unless factually accurate . Do not promise specific performance . Use hedging language when appropriate . check_prompt : | Does this text contain unsupported claims or absolute statements ? Flag : 1. "always" , "never" , "guarantee" , "promise" - unless verifiable 2. Performance claims without evidence 3. Superlatives like "best" , "fastest" , "most secure" without qualification examples : violations : - text : "This solution guarantees 100 % u ptime." fix : "This solution is designed for high availability." reason : "Cannot guarantee 100 % u ptime" - text : "Our product is the most secure option available." fix : "Our product includes multiple security features." reason : "Unverifiable superlative" - text : "This will never fail." fix : "This is designed to handle common failure scenarios." reason : "Absolute claim" acceptable : - text : "In our tests, the system achieved 99.9 % u ptime." reason : "Qualified with 'in our tests'" - text : "This feature helps improve performance." reason : "Hedged language ('helps improve')" # ============================================================ # VERB TENSE (from verbs_rule.py) # ============================================================ verb_tense : id : "verb_tense" category : "language_and_grammar" enabled : true severity : "medium" description : | Checks for inappropriate verb tense usage in technical documentation . Procedures should use present tense and imperative mood . ibm_style_reference : pages : [ 113 , 114 , 115 , 116 ] section : "Language and grammar > Verbs" summary : | Use present tense for procedures . Use imperative mood for instructions . Future tense ( "will" ) is often unnecessary in technical docs . Past tense is appropriate for release notes and changelogs . check_prompt : | Is the verb tense appropriate for this context ? - Procedures : present tense , imperative mood ( "Click" , not "You will click" ) - Descriptions : present tense ( "The system stores" , not "The system will store" ) - Release notes : past tense is OK ( "Fixed" , "Added" ) examples : violations : - text : "You will click the Save button." context : "procedure" fix : "Click the Save button." reason : "Use imperative in procedures" - text : "The system will display an error message." context : "description" fix : "The system displays an error message." reason : "Use present tense for descriptions" acceptable : - text : "Fixed an issue where uploads failed." context : "release notes" reason : "Past tense appropriate for release notes" [/code] Python Implementation ¶ LLM Style Checker ¶ The LLM style checker is at rules/language_and_grammar/llm_style_checker.py : [code] """ LLM Style Checker Evaluates content against LLM-based style rules using Gemini. """ import yaml import json import logging from pathlib import Path from typing import List , Dict , Any , Optional from dataclasses import dataclass , field logger = logging . getLogger ( __name__ ) @dataclass class StyleViolation : """A detected style violation.""" rule_id : str text : str sentence : str message : str suggestion : str severity : str confidence : float ibm_style_ref : str = "" @dataclass class CheckResult : """Result of checking a document.""" violations : List [ StyleViolation ] = field ( default_factory = list ) tokens_used : int = 0 model : str = "" class LLMStyleChecker : """ Checks content against LLM-based style rules. Usage: checker = LLMStyleChecker() result = checker.check(text, context={'doc_type': 'procedure'}) for violation in result.violations: print(f"{violation.rule_id}: {violation.message}") """ def __init__ ( self , rules_file : Optional [ Path ] = None , api_key : Optional [ str ] = None , model : str = "gemini-1.5-flash" ): """ Initialize the checker. Args: rules_file: Path to llm_style_rules.yaml api_key: Gemini API key (or set GEMINI_API_KEY env var) model: Gemini model to use """ if rules_file is None : rules_file = Path ( __file__ ) . parent / "llm_style_rules.yaml" self . rules = self . _load_rules ( rules_file ) self . model = model self . api_key = api_key or self . _get_api_key () logger . info ( f "LLMStyleChecker initialized with { len ( self . rules ) } rules" ) def _load_rules ( self , rules_file : Path ) -> Dict [ str , Dict ]: """Load rules from YAML file.""" if not rules_file . exists (): raise FileNotFoundError ( f "Rules file not found: { rules_file } " ) with open ( rules_file , 'r' , encoding = 'utf-8' ) as f : data = yaml . safe_load ( f ) return { rule_id : rule_config for rule_id , rule_config in data . get ( 'rules' , {}) . items () if rule_config . get ( 'enabled' , True ) } def _get_api_key ( self ) -> str : """Get API key from environment.""" import os key = os . environ . get ( 'GEMINI_API_KEY' , '' ) if not key : raise ValueError ( "GEMINI_API_KEY not set" ) return key def check ( self , text : str , context : Optional [ Dict [ str , Any ]] = None , rules : Optional [ List [ str ]] = None ) -> CheckResult : """ Check text against LLM style rules. Args: text: The content to check context: Optional context (doc_type, block_type, etc.) rules: Optional list of rule IDs to check (default: all enabled) Returns: CheckResult with violations and metadata """ if not text . strip (): return CheckResult () context = context or {} # Skip code blocks if context . get ( 'block_type' ) in [ 'listing' , 'literal' , 'code_block' ]: return CheckResult () # Filter rules rules_to_check = self . rules if rules : rules_to_check = { k : v for k , v in self . rules . items () if k in rules } # Build prompt prompt = self . _build_prompt ( text , context , rules_to_check ) # Call Gemini response = self . _call_gemini ( prompt ) # Parse response violations = self . _parse_response ( response , text ) return CheckResult ( violations = violations , tokens_used = len ( prompt . split ()) + len ( response . split ()), # Rough estimate model = self . model ) def _build_prompt ( self , text : str , context : Dict [ str , Any ], rules : Dict [ str , Dict ] ) -> str : """Build the Gemini prompt.""" rules_section = [] for rule_id , rule in rules . items (): rule_text = f """ ### { rule_id } { rule . get ( 'description' , '' ) } **IBM Style Reference**: { rule . get ( 'ibm_style_reference' , {}) . get ( 'summary' , '' ) } **Check**: { rule . get ( 'check_prompt' , '' ) } """ rules_section . append ( rule_text ) context_info = "" if context : context_info = f """ ## DOCUMENT CONTEXT - Document type: { context . get ( 'doc_type' , 'unknown' ) } - Block type: { context . get ( 'block_type' , 'paragraph' ) } - Section: { context . get ( 'section_title' , 'unknown' ) } """ prompt = f """You are an IBM Style Guide expert reviewing technical documentation. ## TASK Review the following text for style violations. Check ONLY the rules listed below. Be conservative - only flag clear violations, not borderline cases. { context_info } ## RULES TO CHECK { chr ( 10 ) . join ( rules_section ) } ## TEXT TO REVIEW ``` { text } ``` ## RESPONSE FORMAT Return a JSON array of violations (empty array if none): ```json [ {{ "rule_id": "passive_voice", "text": "the exact problematic text", "sentence": "the full sentence containing the issue", "message": "brief explanation of the issue", "suggestion": "the corrected text", "severity": "low|medium|high", "confidence": 0.0-1.0 }} ] ``` IMPORTANT: - Only flag clear violations (confidence > 0.7) - Consider the document context - Return valid JSON only - Empty array [] if no violations found """ return prompt def _call_gemini ( self , prompt : str ) -> str : """Call Gemini API.""" import requests url = f "https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/models/ { self . model } :generateContent" headers = { "Content-Type" : "application/json" , } data = { "contents" : [{ "parts" : [{ "text" : prompt }]}], "generationConfig" : { "temperature" : 0.1 , # Low temperature for consistency "topP" : 0.8 , "maxOutputTokens" : 2048 , } } response = requests . post ( f " { url } ?key= { self . api_key } " , headers = headers , json = data , timeout = 30 ) if response . status_code != 200 : logger . error ( f "Gemini API error: { response . status_code } - { response . text } " ) return "[]" result = response . json () try : return result [ 'candidates' ][ 0 ][ 'content' ][ 'parts' ][ 0 ][ 'text' ] except ( KeyError , IndexError ): logger . error ( f "Unexpected Gemini response format: { result } " ) return "[]" def _parse_response ( self , response : str , original_text : str ) -> List [ StyleViolation ]: """Parse Gemini response into violations.""" import re # Extract JSON from response json_match = re . search ( r '\[[\s\S]*\]' , response ) if not json_match : return [] try : data = json . loads ( json_match . group ()) except json . JSONDecodeError : logger . error ( f "Failed to parse Gemini response: { response [: 200 ] } " ) return [] violations = [] for item in data : if not isinstance ( item , dict ): continue # Filter by confidence confidence = item . get ( 'confidence' , 0.5 ) if confidence < 0.7 : continue violation = StyleViolation ( rule_id = item . get ( 'rule_id' , 'unknown' ), text = item . get ( 'text' , '' ), sentence = item . get ( 'sentence' , '' ), message = item . get ( 'message' , '' ), suggestion = item . get ( 'suggestion' , '' ), severity = item . get ( 'severity' , 'medium' ), confidence = confidence , ibm_style_ref = self . rules . get ( item . get ( 'rule_id' , '' ), {}) . get ( 'ibm_style_reference' , {} ) . get ( 'section' , '' ) ) violations . append ( violation ) return violations # Convenience function def check_style ( text : str , context : Optional [ Dict ] = None ) -> CheckResult : """Quick style check using default configuration.""" checker = LLMStyleChecker () return checker . check ( text , context ) [/code] Integration with Existing System ¶ Hybrid Rule Runner ¶ Modify your main analysis to use both coded and LLM rules: [code] """ Hybrid Rule Runner Combines deterministic coded rules with LLM-based rules. """ from typing import List , Dict , Any from llm_rules.llm_style_checker import LLMStyleChecker , CheckResult # Coded rules (keep these) CODED_RULES = [ 'spelling' , 'inclusive_language' , 'abbreviations' , 'terminology' , 'prefixes' , 'plurals' , 'possessives' , ] # LLM rules (new approach) LLM_RULES = [ 'passive_voice' , 'verb_tense' , 'articles' , 'pronouns' , 'anthropomorphism' , 'conjunctions' , 'prepositions' , 'adverbs_only' , 'unsupported_claims' , ] class HybridRuleRunner : """Runs both coded and LLM rules.""" def __init__ ( self ): self . coded_rules = self . _load_coded_rules () self . llm_checker = LLMStyleChecker () def _load_coded_rules ( self ): """Load deterministic coded rules.""" from rules.language_and_grammar import ( SpellingRule , InclusiveLanguageRule , AbbreviationsRule , TerminologyRule , PrefixesRule , PluralsRule , PossessivesRule ) return { 'spelling' : SpellingRule (), 'inclusive_language' : InclusiveLanguageRule (), 'abbreviations' : AbbreviationsRule (), 'terminology' : TerminologyRule (), 'prefixes' : PrefixesRule (), 'plurals' : PluralsRule (), 'possessives' : PossessivesRule (), } def analyze ( self , text : str , sentences : List [ str ], nlp , context : Dict = None ) -> List [ Dict [ str , Any ]]: """ Run both coded and LLM rules on the text. Args: text: Full text to analyze sentences: List of sentences nlp: SpaCy nlp object context: Block context Returns: Combined list of errors from all rules """ errors = [] context = context or {} # 1. Run coded rules (fast, deterministic) for rule_id , rule in self . coded_rules . items (): try : rule_errors = rule . analyze ( text , sentences , nlp , context ) errors . extend ( rule_errors ) except Exception as e : logger . error ( f "Error in coded rule { rule_id } : { e } " ) # 2. Run LLM rules (slower, judgment-based) try : llm_result = self . llm_checker . check ( text , context ) for violation in llm_result . violations : errors . append ({ 'type' : violation . rule_id , 'message' : violation . message , 'sentence' : violation . sentence , 'suggestions' : [ violation . suggestion ] if violation . suggestion else [], 'severity' : violation . severity , 'evidence_score' : violation . confidence , 'flagged_text' : violation . text , 'source' : 'llm' # Mark as LLM-generated }) except Exception as e : logger . error ( f "Error in LLM rules: { e } " ) return errors [/code] Token Usage and Cost ¶ Estimated Token Usage ¶ Component Input Tokens Output Tokens Total System prompt + rules ~2,000 - 2,000 Text chunk (avg paragraph) ~300 - 300 Response (violations) - ~500 500 Per API call ~2,300 ~500 ~2,800 Cost Estimates (Gemini 1.5 Flash) ¶ Document Size API Calls Total Tokens Cost (USD) Small (10 paragraphs) 10 ~28K ~$0.002 Medium (50 paragraphs) 50 ~140K ~$0.01 Large (200 paragraphs) 200 ~560K ~$0.04 Optimization Strategies ¶ 1. Batch paragraphs : Send 5-10 paragraphs per API call 2. Cache results : Cache violations for unchanged content 3. Skip code blocks : Don't send code to LLM 4. Confidence threshold : Only flag high-confidence violations (>0.7) Adding New LLM Rules ¶ To add a new rule, simply add it to llm_style_rules.yaml : [code] new_rule_name: id: "new_rule_name" category: "language_and_grammar" enabled: true severity: "medium" description: | What this rule checks for. ibm_style_reference: pages: [XX, YY] section: "Section > Subsection" summary: | What the IBM Style Guide says about this. check_prompt: | The specific question to ask the LLM. examples: violations: - text: "example bad text" fix: "example good text" acceptable: - text: "example acceptable text" reason: "why it's acceptable" [/code] No Python code changes required! Migration Completed ¶ __ | We chose direct migration over parallel running. Rationale: * Parallel running = double maintenance burden * Old code would rot and become a source of confusion * If LLM fails, we skip judgment rules (graceful degradation), not crash ---|--- Fallback Strategy ¶ If LLM API is unavailable: [code] def check_llm_rules(text, context): try: return llm_checker.check(text, context) except (APIError, Timeout): logger.warning("LLM unavailable, skipping judgment rules") return [] # Skip, don't fail - coded rules still run [/code] Summary ¶ Rule Distribution ¶ Coded Rules (Keep) LLM Rules (New) spelling passive_voice inclusive_language verb_tense abbreviations articles terminology pronouns prefixes anthropomorphism plurals conjunctions possessives prepositions capitalization (partial) adverbs_only contractions (detection) unsupported_claims What We Deleted ¶ Module Files Lines Deleted rules/language_and_grammar/ 8 files ~9,211 ambiguity/ entire directory ~4,250 Total 12+ files ~13,461 Benefits ¶ * No more 2000+ line Python files for judgment rules * Rule refinement = YAML edit , not Python debugging * Writers can understand and modify rules - no code knowledge needed * LLM has IBM Style Guide knowledge - better context understanding * Consistent behavior - LLM applies rules uniformly * One system to maintain - no parallel codebases Costs ¶ * LLM dependency - requires API access * ~$0.01-0.05 per document - Gemini 1.5 Flash pricing * 500ms-2s latency per check (vs 10ms for coded rules) When LLM is Unavailable ¶ Judgment rules are skipped , not failed. Coded word-list rules still run. This is acceptable because: * Word-list rules catch the most obvious issues * Judgment rules are for nuanced cases * Better to skip than crash the system Last updated 2025-12-20 01:54:23 +0530

docs.title: LLM-Based Style Rules Implementation Guide

docs.text: This guide explains the hybrid architecture for style checking: deterministic rules (coded) for simple pattern matching, and LLM-based rules for judgment-intensive checks. __ | This approach reduces maintenance burden by moving complex, context-dependent rules to LLM evaluation while keeping simple word-list rules as fast, coded checks. Key Decision : We DELETE the old coded rules for judgment-intensive checks. No parallel running, no backup. One system to maintain. Location : All LLM rules are in rules/language_and_grammar/ : * llm_style_checker.py - The Gemini-powered style checker * config/llm_style_rules.yaml - Rule definitions with IBM Style Guide references ---|---
