docs.text: Criteria Example Why Deterministic Finite word list "colour" → "color" Simple lookup, no context needed Binary pattern "URL's" → "URLs" Regex can handle it State tracking "Define abbreviation on first use" Stateful but deterministic Format checking "I.B.M." → "IBM" Pattern matching
docs.title: When to Use Coded Rules (Deterministic)

docs.text: Criteria Example Why LLM Needed Context changes meaning "The file is opened" - passive OK in some contexts Release notes vs procedures differ Semantic understanding "it" pronoun - what does it refer to? Needs to understand antecedent Style judgment Starting sentence with "And" Sometimes acceptable for emphasis Multiple valid options "a" vs "the" article choice Depends on whether item was introduced
docs.title: When to Use LLM Rules (Judgment)

docs.text: Rule Lines Type Rationale spelling_rule.py 718 ✅ CODED Word list: "colour"→"color" inclusive_language_rule.py 810 ✅ CODED Word list: "blacklist"→"blocklist" abbreviations_rule.py 966 ✅ CODED Latin abbrev list + first-use tracking terminology_rule.py 769 ✅ CODED IBM Terminology database lookup prefixes_rule.py 1048 ✅ CODED Word list: "pre-install"→"preinstall" plurals_rule.py 1514 ✅ CODED Pattern: apostrophe in plurals possessives_rule.py 710 ✅ CODED Apostrophe rules verbs_rule.py 2470 🤖 LLM Passive voice, tense - context dependent passive_voice_analyzer.py 1347 🤖 LLM When is passive acceptable? articles_rule.py 1239 🤖 LLM "a" vs "the" - context dependent pronouns_rule.py 584 🤖 LLM Ambiguous pronoun references anthropomorphism_rule.py 482 🤖 LLM "system knows" vs "AI learns" conjunctions_rule.py 1439 🤖 LLM Starting with "And/But" - style judgment prepositions_rule.py 1204 🤖 LLM Ending with preposition - often fine adverbs_only_rule.py 446 🤖 LLM "only" placement ambiguity
docs.title: Language and Grammar Rules

docs.text: Create llm_style_rules.yaml : [code] # LLM-Based Style Rules Configuration # These rules require contextual judgment and are evaluated by Gemini version : "1.0" model : "gemini-1.5-flash" rules : # ============================================================ # VERBS AND VOICE # ============================================================ passive_voice : id : "passive_voice" category : "language_and_grammar" enabled : true severity : "medium" description : | Identifies inappropriate passive voice constructions . Active voice is preferred in technical documentation . ibm_style_reference : pages : [ 113 , 114 , 115 , 116 ] section : "Language and grammar > Verbs" summary : | Use active voice in most cases . Passive voice is acceptable when : - The actor is unknown or unimportant - In release notes describing past events - When emphasizing the action over the actor check_prompt : | Is this sentence using passive voice inappropriately ? Consider : Is this a procedure ( should be active ) or a description where passive might be acceptable ? examples : violations : - text : "The file should be opened by the user." context : "procedure step" fix : "Open the file." - text : "The configuration is recommended to be updated." context : "procedure" fix : "Update the configuration." acceptable : - text : "The bug was fixed in version 2.0." context : "release notes" reason : "Passive acceptable in release notes" - text : "The data is stored in the database." context : "description" reason : "Actor (system) is obvious" # ============================================================ # ARTICLES # ============================================================ articles : id : "articles" category : "language_and_grammar" enabled : true severity : "low" description : | Checks for missing , incorrect , or inconsistent article usage ( a / an / the ) . ibm_style_reference : pages : [ 91 , 92 ] section : "Language and grammar > Articles" summary : | Use "a" before consonant sounds , "an" before vowel sounds . This applies to pronunciation , not spelling : - "an SQL query" ( ess - queue - el ) - "a URL" ( you - are - el ) - "an hour" ( silent h ) check_prompt : | Is the article usage correct in this sentence ? Check for : 1. Missing articles where needed 2. Wrong article ( a / an based on sound ) 3. Unnecessary articles examples : violations : - text : "Deploy pod to the cluster." fix : "Deploy the pod to the cluster." reason : "Missing article before 'pod'" - text : "This is a SQL query." fix : "This is an SQL query." reason : "SQL is pronounced 'ess-queue-el' (vowel sound)" acceptable : - text : "Kubernetes uses pods for deployment." reason : "Generic/plural usage, no article needed" # ============================================================ # PRONOUNS # ============================================================ pronouns : id : "pronouns" category : "language_and_grammar" enabled : true severity : "medium" description : | Identifies ambiguous pronoun references where the antecedent is unclear . ibm_style_reference : pages : [ 109 , 110 , 111 ] section : "Language and grammar > Pronouns" summary : | Ensure pronouns have clear antecedents . "This" and "that" at the start of sentences should be followed by a noun . Avoid ambiguous pronoun references . check_prompt : | Does this pronoun have a clear antecedent ? Flag if : 1. "It" could refer to multiple things 2. "This" or "That" starts a sentence without a noun 3. The referent is ambiguous examples : violations : - text : "Configure the server and the database. It should be restarted." fix : "Configure the server and the database. The server should be restarted." reason : "'It' could refer to server or database" - text : "This is important for security." fix : "This configuration is important for security." reason : "'This' needs a noun" acceptable : - text : "Create a pod. It runs your application." reason : "Clear single antecedent (pod)" # ============================================================ # ANTHROPOMORPHISM # ============================================================ anthropomorphism : id : "anthropomorphism" category : "language_and_grammar" enabled : true severity : "low" description : | Flags inappropriate attribution of human characteristics to software . ibm_style_reference : pages : [ 90 ] section : "Language and grammar > Anthropomorphism" summary : | Do not attribute human characteristics to software or hardware . Write "the system processes" not "the system thinks" . Avoid : knows , thinks , wants , sees , remembers , decides . check_prompt : | Does this sentence inappropriately attribute human qualities to software ? Note : AI / ML systems CAN "learn" and "predict" - these are technical terms . examples : violations : - text : "The application knows your preferences." fix : "The application stores your preferences." - text : "The system wants you to restart." fix : "The system requires a restart." acceptable : - text : "The AI model learns from the training data." reason : "'Learns' is a valid ML term" - text : "The recommendation engine predicts user behavior." reason : "'Predicts' is a valid ML term" # ============================================================ # CONJUNCTIONS # ============================================================ conjunctions : id : "conjunctions" category : "language_and_grammar" enabled : true severity : "low" description : | Checks for sentences starting with conjunctions and parallel structure issues . ibm_style_reference : pages : [ 101 , 102 ] section : "Language and grammar > Conjunctions" summary : | It 's acceptable to start sentences with "And" or "But" for emphasis, but use sparingly . Ensure parallel structure with coordinating conjunctions . check_prompt : | Is the conjunction usage appropriate here ? 1. Starting with And / But - is it for emphasis or just lazy writing ? 2. Is parallel structure maintained ? examples : violations : - text : "And then you click the button." fix : "Then click the button." reason : "Unnecessary conjunction in procedure" acceptable : - text : "But this approach has limitations." reason : "Intentional emphasis after contrasting point" # ============================================================ # PREPOSITIONS # ============================================================ prepositions : id : "prepositions" category : "language_and_grammar" enabled : true severity : "low" description : | Checks for awkward prepositional phrases and ending sentences with prepositions . ibm_style_reference : pages : [ 108 , 109 ] section : "Language and grammar > Prepositions" summary : | It 's acceptable to end sentences with prepositions when natural. Avoid excessive prepositional phrases . check_prompt : | Is the preposition usage natural and clear ? Ending with a preposition is usually fine in technical docs . Flag only if it sounds awkward or unclear . examples : acceptable : - text : "Select the file you want to work on." reason : "Natural phrasing, ending with preposition is fine" - text : "This is the server the app connects to." reason : "Clear and natural" # ============================================================ # ADVERBS - ONLY # ============================================================ adverbs_only : id : "adverbs_only" category : "language_and_grammar" enabled : true severity : "low" description : | Checks for ambiguous placement of the word "only" . ibm_style_reference : pages : [ 89 ] section : "Language and grammar > Adverbs - only" summary : | Place "only" immediately before the word or phrase it modifies . Incorrect placement can change meaning . check_prompt : | Is "only" placed correctly to convey the intended meaning ? "You can only edit" vs "You can edit only" have different meanings . examples : violations : - text : "You can only edit files in this directory." ambiguity : "Does 'only' modify 'edit' or 'this directory'?" options : - "You can edit only files in this directory." - "You can edit files only in this directory." acceptable : - text : "You only need to restart the service." reason : "Clear meaning - restart is the only required action" # ============================================================ # UNSUPPORTED CLAIMS (from ambiguity module) # ============================================================ unsupported_claims : id : "unsupported_claims" category : "legal_information" enabled : true severity : "high" description : | Detects unsupported claims , promises , and absolute statements that could be legally problematic or misleading . ibm_style_reference : pages : [ 279 , 280 ] section : "Legal information > Claims and recommendations" summary : | Do not make unsubstantiated claims . Avoid absolute terms like "always" and "never" unless factually accurate . Do not promise specific performance . Use hedging language when appropriate . check_prompt : | Does this text contain unsupported claims or absolute statements ? Flag : 1. "always" , "never" , "guarantee" , "promise" - unless verifiable 2. Performance claims without evidence 3. Superlatives like "best" , "fastest" , "most secure" without qualification examples : violations : - text : "This solution guarantees 100 % u ptime." fix : "This solution is designed for high availability." reason : "Cannot guarantee 100 % u ptime" - text : "Our product is the most secure option available." fix : "Our product includes multiple security features." reason : "Unverifiable superlative" - text : "This will never fail." fix : "This is designed to handle common failure scenarios." reason : "Absolute claim" acceptable : - text : "In our tests, the system achieved 99.9 % u ptime." reason : "Qualified with 'in our tests'" - text : "This feature helps improve performance." reason : "Hedged language ('helps improve')" # ============================================================ # VERB TENSE (from verbs_rule.py) # ============================================================ verb_tense : id : "verb_tense" category : "language_and_grammar" enabled : true severity : "medium" description : | Checks for inappropriate verb tense usage in technical documentation . Procedures should use present tense and imperative mood . ibm_style_reference : pages : [ 113 , 114 , 115 , 116 ] section : "Language and grammar > Verbs" summary : | Use present tense for procedures . Use imperative mood for instructions . Future tense ( "will" ) is often unnecessary in technical docs . Past tense is appropriate for release notes and changelogs . check_prompt : | Is the verb tense appropriate for this context ? - Procedures : present tense , imperative mood ( "Click" , not "You will click" ) - Descriptions : present tense ( "The system stores" , not "The system will store" ) - Release notes : past tense is OK ( "Fixed" , "Added" ) examples : violations : - text : "You will click the Save button." context : "procedure" fix : "Click the Save button." reason : "Use imperative in procedures" - text : "The system will display an error message." context : "description" fix : "The system displays an error message." reason : "Use present tense for descriptions" acceptable : - text : "Fixed an issue where uploads failed." context : "release notes" reason : "Past tense appropriate for release notes" [/code]
docs.title: Rule Definition Format

docs.text: The LLM style checker is at rules/language_and_grammar/llm_style_checker.py : [code] """ LLM Style Checker Evaluates content against LLM-based style rules using Gemini. """ import yaml import json import logging from pathlib import Path from typing import List , Dict , Any , Optional from dataclasses import dataclass , field logger = logging . getLogger ( __name__ ) @dataclass class StyleViolation : """A detected style violation.""" rule_id : str text : str sentence : str message : str suggestion : str severity : str confidence : float ibm_style_ref : str = "" @dataclass class CheckResult : """Result of checking a document.""" violations : List [ StyleViolation ] = field ( default_factory = list ) tokens_used : int = 0 model : str = "" class LLMStyleChecker : """ Checks content against LLM-based style rules. Usage: checker = LLMStyleChecker() result = checker.check(text, context={'doc_type': 'procedure'}) for violation in result.violations: print(f"{violation.rule_id}: {violation.message}") """ def __init__ ( self , rules_file : Optional [ Path ] = None , api_key : Optional [ str ] = None , model : str = "gemini-1.5-flash" ): """ Initialize the checker. Args: rules_file: Path to llm_style_rules.yaml api_key: Gemini API key (or set GEMINI_API_KEY env var) model: Gemini model to use """ if rules_file is None : rules_file = Path ( __file__ ) . parent / "llm_style_rules.yaml" self . rules = self . _load_rules ( rules_file ) self . model = model self . api_key = api_key or self . _get_api_key () logger . info ( f "LLMStyleChecker initialized with { len ( self . rules ) } rules" ) def _load_rules ( self , rules_file : Path ) -> Dict [ str , Dict ]: """Load rules from YAML file.""" if not rules_file . exists (): raise FileNotFoundError ( f "Rules file not found: { rules_file } " ) with open ( rules_file , 'r' , encoding = 'utf-8' ) as f : data = yaml . safe_load ( f ) return { rule_id : rule_config for rule_id , rule_config in data . get ( 'rules' , {}) . items () if rule_config . get ( 'enabled' , True ) } def _get_api_key ( self ) -> str : """Get API key from environment.""" import os key = os . environ . get ( 'GEMINI_API_KEY' , '' ) if not key : raise ValueError ( "GEMINI_API_KEY not set" ) return key def check ( self , text : str , context : Optional [ Dict [ str , Any ]] = None , rules : Optional [ List [ str ]] = None ) -> CheckResult : """ Check text against LLM style rules. Args: text: The content to check context: Optional context (doc_type, block_type, etc.) rules: Optional list of rule IDs to check (default: all enabled) Returns: CheckResult with violations and metadata """ if not text . strip (): return CheckResult () context = context or {} # Skip code blocks if context . get ( 'block_type' ) in [ 'listing' , 'literal' , 'code_block' ]: return CheckResult () # Filter rules rules_to_check = self . rules if rules : rules_to_check = { k : v for k , v in self . rules . items () if k in rules } # Build prompt prompt = self . _build_prompt ( text , context , rules_to_check ) # Call Gemini response = self . _call_gemini ( prompt ) # Parse response violations = self . _parse_response ( response , text ) return CheckResult ( violations = violations , tokens_used = len ( prompt . split ()) + len ( response . split ()), # Rough estimate model = self . model ) def _build_prompt ( self , text : str , context : Dict [ str , Any ], rules : Dict [ str , Dict ] ) -> str : """Build the Gemini prompt.""" rules_section = [] for rule_id , rule in rules . items (): rule_text = f """ ### { rule_id } { rule . get ( 'description' , '' ) } **IBM Style Reference**: { rule . get ( 'ibm_style_reference' , {}) . get ( 'summary' , '' ) } **Check**: { rule . get ( 'check_prompt' , '' ) } """ rules_section . append ( rule_text ) context_info = "" if context : context_info = f """ ## DOCUMENT CONTEXT - Document type: { context . get ( 'doc_type' , 'unknown' ) } - Block type: { context . get ( 'block_type' , 'paragraph' ) } - Section: { context . get ( 'section_title' , 'unknown' ) } """ prompt = f """You are an IBM Style Guide expert reviewing technical documentation. ## TASK Review the following text for style violations. Check ONLY the rules listed below. Be conservative - only flag clear violations, not borderline cases. { context_info } ## RULES TO CHECK { chr ( 10 ) . join ( rules_section ) } ## TEXT TO REVIEW ``` { text } ``` ## RESPONSE FORMAT Return a JSON array of violations (empty array if none): ```json [ {{ "rule_id": "passive_voice", "text": "the exact problematic text", "sentence": "the full sentence containing the issue", "message": "brief explanation of the issue", "suggestion": "the corrected text", "severity": "low|medium|high", "confidence": 0.0-1.0 }} ] ``` IMPORTANT: - Only flag clear violations (confidence > 0.7) - Consider the document context - Return valid JSON only - Empty array [] if no violations found """ return prompt def _call_gemini ( self , prompt : str ) -> str : """Call Gemini API.""" import requests url = f "https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/models/ { self . model } :generateContent" headers = { "Content-Type" : "application/json" , } data = { "contents" : [{ "parts" : [{ "text" : prompt }]}], "generationConfig" : { "temperature" : 0.1 , # Low temperature for consistency "topP" : 0.8 , "maxOutputTokens" : 2048 , } } response = requests . post ( f " { url } ?key= { self . api_key } " , headers = headers , json = data , timeout = 30 ) if response . status_code != 200 : logger . error ( f "Gemini API error: { response . status_code } - { response . text } " ) return "[]" result = response . json () try : return result [ 'candidates' ][ 0 ][ 'content' ][ 'parts' ][ 0 ][ 'text' ] except ( KeyError , IndexError ): logger . error ( f "Unexpected Gemini response format: { result } " ) return "[]" def _parse_response ( self , response : str , original_text : str ) -> List [ StyleViolation ]: """Parse Gemini response into violations.""" import re # Extract JSON from response json_match = re . search ( r '\[[\s\S]*\]' , response ) if not json_match : return [] try : data = json . loads ( json_match . group ()) except json . JSONDecodeError : logger . error ( f "Failed to parse Gemini response: { response [: 200 ] } " ) return [] violations = [] for item in data : if not isinstance ( item , dict ): continue # Filter by confidence confidence = item . get ( 'confidence' , 0.5 ) if confidence < 0.7 : continue violation = StyleViolation ( rule_id = item . get ( 'rule_id' , 'unknown' ), text = item . get ( 'text' , '' ), sentence = item . get ( 'sentence' , '' ), message = item . get ( 'message' , '' ), suggestion = item . get ( 'suggestion' , '' ), severity = item . get ( 'severity' , 'medium' ), confidence = confidence , ibm_style_ref = self . rules . get ( item . get ( 'rule_id' , '' ), {}) . get ( 'ibm_style_reference' , {} ) . get ( 'section' , '' ) ) violations . append ( violation ) return violations # Convenience function def check_style ( text : str , context : Optional [ Dict ] = None ) -> CheckResult : """Quick style check using default configuration.""" checker = LLMStyleChecker () return checker . check ( text , context ) [/code]
docs.title: LLM Style Checker

docs.text: Modify your main analysis to use both coded and LLM rules: [code] """ Hybrid Rule Runner Combines deterministic coded rules with LLM-based rules. """ from typing import List , Dict , Any from llm_rules.llm_style_checker import LLMStyleChecker , CheckResult # Coded rules (keep these) CODED_RULES = [ 'spelling' , 'inclusive_language' , 'abbreviations' , 'terminology' , 'prefixes' , 'plurals' , 'possessives' , ] # LLM rules (new approach) LLM_RULES = [ 'passive_voice' , 'verb_tense' , 'articles' , 'pronouns' , 'anthropomorphism' , 'conjunctions' , 'prepositions' , 'adverbs_only' , 'unsupported_claims' , ] class HybridRuleRunner : """Runs both coded and LLM rules.""" def __init__ ( self ): self . coded_rules = self . _load_coded_rules () self . llm_checker = LLMStyleChecker () def _load_coded_rules ( self ): """Load deterministic coded rules.""" from rules.language_and_grammar import ( SpellingRule , InclusiveLanguageRule , AbbreviationsRule , TerminologyRule , PrefixesRule , PluralsRule , PossessivesRule ) return { 'spelling' : SpellingRule (), 'inclusive_language' : InclusiveLanguageRule (), 'abbreviations' : AbbreviationsRule (), 'terminology' : TerminologyRule (), 'prefixes' : PrefixesRule (), 'plurals' : PluralsRule (), 'possessives' : PossessivesRule (), } def analyze ( self , text : str , sentences : List [ str ], nlp , context : Dict = None ) -> List [ Dict [ str , Any ]]: """ Run both coded and LLM rules on the text. Args: text: Full text to analyze sentences: List of sentences nlp: SpaCy nlp object context: Block context Returns: Combined list of errors from all rules """ errors = [] context = context or {} # 1. Run coded rules (fast, deterministic) for rule_id , rule in self . coded_rules . items (): try : rule_errors = rule . analyze ( text , sentences , nlp , context ) errors . extend ( rule_errors ) except Exception as e : logger . error ( f "Error in coded rule { rule_id } : { e } " ) # 2. Run LLM rules (slower, judgment-based) try : llm_result = self . llm_checker . check ( text , context ) for violation in llm_result . violations : errors . append ({ 'type' : violation . rule_id , 'message' : violation . message , 'sentence' : violation . sentence , 'suggestions' : [ violation . suggestion ] if violation . suggestion else [], 'severity' : violation . severity , 'evidence_score' : violation . confidence , 'flagged_text' : violation . text , 'source' : 'llm' # Mark as LLM-generated }) except Exception as e : logger . error ( f "Error in LLM rules: { e } " ) return errors [/code]

docs.text: 1. Batch paragraphs : Send 5-10 paragraphs per API call 2. Cache results : Cache violations for unchanged content 3. Skip code blocks : Don't send code to LLM 4. Confidence threshold : Only flag high-confidence violations (>0.7)
docs.title: Optimization Strategies

docs.text: To add a new rule, simply add it to llm_style_rules.yaml : [code] new_rule_name: id: "new_rule_name" category: "language_and_grammar" enabled: true severity: "medium" description: | What this rule checks for. ibm_style_reference: pages: [XX, YY] section: "Section > Subsection" summary: | What the IBM Style Guide says about this. check_prompt: | The specific question to ask the LLM. examples: violations: - text: "example bad text" fix: "example good text" acceptable: - text: "example acceptable text" reason: "why it's acceptable" [/code] No Python code changes required!
docs.title: Adding New LLM Rules

docs.text: __ | We chose direct migration over parallel running. Rationale: * Parallel running = double maintenance burden * Old code would rot and become a source of confusion * If LLM fails, we skip judgment rules (graceful degradation), not crash ---|---
docs.title: Migration Completed

docs.text: If LLM API is unavailable: [code] def check_llm_rules(text, context): try: return llm_checker.check(text, context) except (APIError, Timeout): logger.warning("LLM unavailable, skipping judgment rules") return [] # Skip, don't fail - coded rules still run [/code]
docs.title: Fallback Strategy

docs.text: Coded Rules (Keep) LLM Rules (New) spelling passive_voice inclusive_language verb_tense abbreviations articles terminology pronouns prefixes anthropomorphism plurals conjunctions possessives prepositions capitalization (partial) adverbs_only contractions (detection) unsupported_claims
docs.title: Rule Distribution

docs.text: Module Files Lines Deleted rules/language_and_grammar/ 8 files ~9,211 ambiguity/ entire directory ~4,250 Total 12+ files ~13,461
docs.title: What We Deleted

docs.text: * No more 2000+ line Python files for judgment rules * Rule refinement = YAML edit , not Python debugging * Writers can understand and modify rules - no code knowledge needed * LLM has IBM Style Guide knowledge - better context understanding * Consistent behavior - LLM applies rules uniformly * One system to maintain - no parallel codebases
docs.title: Benefits

docs.text: * LLM dependency - requires API access * ~$0.01-0.05 per document - Gemini 1.5 Flash pricing * 500ms-2s latency per check (vs 10ms for coded rules)
docs.title: Costs

docs.text: Judgment rules are skipped , not failed. Coded word-list rules still run. This is acceptable because: * Word-list rules catch the most obvious issues * Judgment rules are for nuanced cases * Better to skip than crash the system Last updated 2025-12-20 01:54:23 +0530
docs.title: When LLM is Unavailable

docs.text: AI-Powered Testing Agent Guide ¶ Understand how the AI-powered testing agent works and how it helps maintain quality. Overview ¶ The Content Editorial Assistant uses a hybrid agent approach combining: * Simple Agent (GitLab CI) - Reliable test execution * AI Agent (Your configured AI) - Intelligent analysis and insights Architecture ¶ [code] ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ GitLab CI (Simple Agent) │ │ • Executes all tests daily │ │ • Collects results │ │ • Stores data │ └─────────────┬────────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Test Runner │ │ testing_agent/test_runner.py │ │ • Runs 9 test categories │ │ • Collects metrics │ └─────────────┬────────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ AI Analyzer (Smart Agent) │ │ testing_agent/ai_test_analyzer.py │ │ • Detects patterns │ │ • Identifies root causes │ │ • Prioritizes issues │ │ • Generates insights │ └─────────────┬────────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Report Generator │ │ testing_agent/report_generator.py │ │ • Creates HTML/PDF reports │ │ • Generates charts │ │ • Lists action items │ └──────────────────────────────────────────┘ [/code] What the AI Agent Does ¶ Pattern Detection ¶ The AI analyzes test failures to find common patterns: [code] { "test_database_connection": "ConnectionError: pool exhausted", "test_concurrent_access": "ConnectionError: pool exhausted", "test_transaction_rollback": "ConnectionError: pool exhausted" } [/code] AI Analysis: [code] 🧠 Pattern Detected: 3 tests failing with identical ConnectionError Root Cause: Database connection pool exhaustion Likely Issue: Test fixtures not properly closing connections Recommended Actions: 1. Add connection cleanup in test teardown (HIGH PRIORITY) 2. Increase pool size in test config (MEDIUM) 3. Add connection leak detection (LOW) Estimated Fix Time: 2 hours [/code] Regression Detection ¶ Automatically identifies tests that passed yesterday but fail today: [code] 🔴 NEW FAILURES (regressions): 1. test_holistic_rewrite Error : Confidence score to o low ( 0.65 < 0.80 ) Priority : HIGH Estimate : 2 hours Action : Check line 245 confidence calculation [/code] Performance Monitoring ¶ Tracks test execution time over 30 days: [code] Test Duration: ↓ 5.2% faster ✅ Average Latency: 1.2s (stable) Slowest Test: test_large_document (45s) ⚠️ Alert: test_api_response_time 20% slower [/code] Smart Prioritization ¶ AI prioritizes issues based on impact: Priority Issue Type Action Needed P1 - HIGH Blocking release, new regressions Fix today P2 - MEDIUM Performance degradation Fix this week P3 - LOW Flaky tests, minor issues Fix when convenient Daily Report Contents ¶ Every test run generates a comprehensive report: 1. Executive Summary ¶ [code] Status: 🟢 HEALTHY Pass Rate: 96% (382/398 tests) Duration: 4m 32s Health Score: A+ (97.5/100) [/code] 2. Test Results Breakdown ¶ Interactive pie chart showing: * Passed tests (green) * Failed tests (red) * Skipped tests (yellow) 3. Regressions ¶ List of tests that previously passed but now fail. 4. Performance Trends ¶ 30-day chart showing: * Test execution time * Pass rate trend * Performance metrics 5. AI-Generated Insights ¶ Intelligent analysis of issues: [code] 🧠 5 ISSUES IDENTIFIED: [1] Database connection pool exhaustion → Add cleanup in test teardown [2] Regex compilation in hot path → Cache compiled patterns [3] Flaky websocket test needs timeout → Increase timeout from 5s to 10s [/code] 6. Prioritized Action Items ¶ Clear, actionable steps: [code] ✅ WHAT TO DO TODAY: 1. Fix test_holistic_rewrite ( HIGH - 2 h ) → Check line 245 confidence calculation 2. Optimize SurgicalSnippetProcessor ( MEDIUM - 4 h ) → Profile hot paths , cache regex 3. Stabilize websocket test ( LOW - 1 h ) → Increase timeout configuration [/code] Test Coverage ¶ What's Tested ¶ Module Coverage ambiguity/ Ambiguity detection rules app_modules/ API routes, websockets, PDF generation database/ DAO, models, services error_consolidation/ Error merging and prioritization models/ Model factory, manager rewriter/ Core rewriting engine style_analyzer/ Style analysis, readability structural_parsing/ Format detection, parsers rules/ All 111 editorial rules Feature-Test Mapping ¶ Automatically tracks which features have tests: [code] # Check coverage status python -m testing_agent.feature_test_mapper # Output: Total Features: 45 Tested: 38 (84%) Missing Tests: 7 ⚠️ Untested Features: - feature_x in module_y - feature_z in module_w [/code] How Tests Stay In Sync ¶ 1. CI Triggers on Code Changes ¶ [code] Developer commits → CI runs tests → Reports results [/code] 2. Feature-Test Mapping ¶ [code] New feature added → Mapper detects → Alerts "no tests found" [/code] 3. Daily Verification ¶ [code] Daily run → Checks all features → Reports coverage gaps [/code] AI Configuration ¶ The testing agent uses your existing AI configuration: [code] # From your .env file MODEL_PROVIDER=api # Your provider (api/ollama/llamastack) BASE_URL= https://your-api-url.com # Your API endpoint MODEL_ID=mistralai/Mistral-7B # Your model ACCESS_TOKEN=your-token # Your credentials MODEL_TEMPERATURE=0.4 # Your settings [/code] Tip The testing agent automatically detects and uses your configured AI provider. No separate setup needed! Alerts and Notifications ¶ Alert Triggers ¶ Condition Level Action Pass rate < 85% 🔴 CRITICAL Immediate fix needed New regressions 🔴 HIGH Review before release Performance -20% 🟡 WARNING Investigate today Flaky tests > 10% 🟡 WARNING Stabilize this week Configure Notifications ¶ Add to .env : [code] SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL= https://hooks.slack.com/ ... docs.text: Understand how the AI-powered testing agent works and how it helps maintain quality.
docs.title: AI-Powered Testing Agent Guide

docs.text: The Content Editorial Assistant uses a hybrid agent approach combining: * Simple Agent (GitLab CI) - Reliable test execution * AI Agent (Your configured AI) - Intelligent analysis and insights
docs.title: Overview

docs.text: [code] ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ GitLab CI (Simple Agent) │ │ • Executes all tests daily │ │ • Collects results │ │ • Stores data │ └─────────────┬────────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Test Runner │ │ testing_agent/test_runner.py │ │ • Runs 9 test categories │ │ • Collects metrics │ └─────────────┬────────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ AI Analyzer (Smart Agent) │ │ testing_agent/ai_test_analyzer.py │ │ • Detects patterns │ │ • Identifies root causes │ │ • Prioritizes issues │ │ • Generates insights │ └─────────────┬────────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Report Generator │ │ testing_agent/report_generator.py │ │ • Creates HTML/PDF reports │ │ • Generates charts │ │ • Lists action items │ └──────────────────────────────────────────┘ [/code]
docs.title: Architecture

docs.text: The AI analyzes test failures to find common patterns: [code] { "test_database_connection": "ConnectionError: pool exhausted", "test_concurrent_access": "ConnectionError: pool exhausted", "test_transaction_rollback": "ConnectionError: pool exhausted" } [/code] AI Analysis: [code] 🧠 Pattern Detected: 3 tests failing with identical ConnectionError Root Cause: Database connection pool exhaustion Likely Issue: Test fixtures not properly closing connections Recommended Actions: 1. Add connection cleanup in test teardown (HIGH PRIORITY) 2. Increase pool size in test config (MEDIUM) 3. Add connection leak detection (LOW) Estimated Fix Time: 2 hours [/code]
docs.title: Pattern Detection

docs.text: Automatically identifies tests that passed yesterday but fail today: [code] 🔴 NEW FAILURES (regressions): 1. test_holistic_rewrite Error : Confidence score to o low ( 0.65 < 0.80 ) Priority : HIGH Estimate : 2 hours Action : Check line 245 confidence calculation [/code]
docs.title: Regression Detection

docs.text: Tracks test execution time over 30 days: [code] Test Duration: ↓ 5.2% faster ✅ Average Latency: 1.2s (stable) Slowest Test: test_large_document (45s) ⚠️ Alert: test_api_response_time 20% slower [/code]
docs.title: Performance Monitoring

docs.text: AI prioritizes issues based on impact: Priority Issue Type Action Needed P1 - HIGH Blocking release, new regressions Fix today P2 - MEDIUM Performance degradation Fix this week P3 - LOW Flaky tests, minor issues Fix when convenient
docs.title: Smart Prioritization

docs.text: Every test run generates a comprehensive report:
docs.title: Daily Report Contents

docs.text: [code] Status: 🟢 HEALTHY Pass Rate: 96% (382/398 tests) Duration: 4m 32s Health Score: A+ (97.5/100) [/code]
docs.title: 1. Executive Summary

docs.text: Interactive pie chart showing: * Passed tests (green) * Failed tests (red) * Skipped tests (yellow)
docs.title: 2. Test Results Breakdown

docs.text: List of tests that previously passed but now fail.
docs.title: 3. Regressions

docs.text: 30-day chart showing: * Test execution time * Pass rate trend * Performance metrics
docs.title: 4. Performance Trends

docs.text: Intelligent analysis of issues: [code] 🧠 5 ISSUES IDENTIFIED: [1] Database connection pool exhaustion → Add cleanup in test teardown [2] Regex compilation in hot path → Cache compiled patterns [3] Flaky websocket test needs timeout → Increase timeout from 5s to 10s [/code]
docs.title: 5. AI-Generated Insights

docs.text: Clear, actionable steps: [code] ✅ WHAT TO DO TODAY: 1. Fix test_holistic_rewrite ( HIGH - 2 h ) → Check line 245 confidence calculation 2. Optimize SurgicalSnippetProcessor ( MEDIUM - 4 h ) → Profile hot paths , cache regex 3. Stabilize websocket test ( LOW - 1 h ) → Increase timeout configuration [/code]

docs.text: Testing Setup and Quick Start ¶ Learn how to set up and run the comprehensive testing infrastructure for the Content Editorial Assistant. Prerequisites ¶ * Python 3.12+ with virtual environment * Git and GitLab access * Your existing .env configuration Quick Setup (5 Minutes) ¶ 1. Install Test Dependencies ¶ [code] # Activate your virtual environment source venv/bin/activate # Install test packages pip install - r requirements - test . txt # Install Playwright browsers for UI testing playwright install chromium [/code] 2. Run Your First Test ¶ [code] # Run all tests with AI analysis and report python -m testing_agent.test_runner # Or run specific categories pytest tests/unit/ -v # Unit tests only pytest tests/integration/ -v # Integration tests pytest tests/ui/ -v # UI tests with browser [/code] 3. View the Report ¶ [code] # Open the generated HTML report open testing_agent/reports/latest_report.html # Or: firefox testing_agent/reports/latest_report.html [/code] Test Categories ¶ The testing system runs 9 categories of tests: Category Description Typical Duration Unit Fast, isolated component tests 2 min Integration Multi-component workflow tests 5 min API REST endpoint tests 1 min Database Database operations 2 min Frontend Frontend component tests 2 min UI Browser-based tests (Playwright) 3 min WebSocket Real-time communication tests 1 min Validation Validation module tests 3 min Performance Load and stress testing 2 min AI-Powered Analysis ¶ The testing agent uses your existing AI configuration from .env : [code] # Your .env file MODEL_PROVIDER=api # Uses your configured provider BASE_URL= https://your-api-url.com MODEL_ID=mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 ACCESS_TOKEN=your-token [/code] The AI analyzes test results and provides: * Pattern detection (same error across multiple tests) * Root cause suggestions * Prioritized action items * Regression detection * Performance trend analysis Daily Automation (GitLab CI) ¶ Set up automated daily test runs: 1. Go to GitLab → CI/CD → Schedules 2. Click New schedule 3. Set schedule: 0 2 * * * (runs at 2 AM) 4. Add variable: SCHEDULED_JOB=daily_tests 5. Save Tests will run automatically every day and generate reports. Configuration ¶ Create or update .env for testing-specific settings: [code] # Application APP_URL= http://localhost:5000 HEADLESS=true # Test Execution PARALLEL_TESTS=true TEST_TIMEOUT=300 [/code] Note The testing agent reuses your existing MODEL_PROVIDER , BASE_URL , and other model settings from .env . Common Commands ¶ [code] # Run all tests with coverage pytest --cov=. --cov-report=html # Run specific test file pytest tests/unit/rules/test_base_rule.py -v # Collect tests without running pytest --collect-only tests/ # Run tests matching a pattern pytest -k "database" -v # Run with different verbosity pytest -v # Verbose pytest -vv # Very verbose pytest -q # Quiet [/code] Troubleshooting ¶ Import Errors ¶ [code] # Ensure virtual environment is activated source venv/bin/activate # Set PYTHONPATH export PYTHONPATH = "${PYTHONPATH}:$(pwd)" [/code] Missing Dependencies ¶ [code] # Reinstall test dependencies pip install -r requirements-test.txt --force-reinstall [/code] Playwright Errors ¶ [code] # Install system dependencies playwright install-deps chromium # Reinstall browsers playwright install chromium --force [/code] Next Steps ¶ * Learn about agent-driven testing * Write new tests * Return to documentation home Resources ¶ * Main README * Testing Agent Technical Reference * Pytest Documentation * Playwright Documentation Last updated 2025-11-24 22:49:07 +0530

docs.text: AI-assisted writing training guide ¶ Table of Contents * Training overview * Training objectives * Target audience * Estimated training time * Module 1: Understanding AI-assisted writing * 1.1 What is AI-assisted writing? * 1.2 How CEA uses AI * 1.3 AI limitations you must understand * Module 2: Human-in-the-loop principle * 2.1 What is human-in-the-loop? * 2.2 Why human review is essential * 2.3 The review process * Module 3: Using Content Editorial Assistant * 3.1 Accessing CEA * 3.2 Input methods * Uploading documents * Direct text entry * 3.3 Running analysis * 3.4 Understanding results * Error categories * Readability metrics * 3.5 Using AI rewrite feature * 3.6 Generating reports * Module 4: Best practices * 4.1 Before using AI assistance * 4.2 During AI-assisted work * 4.3 After using AI assistance * 4.4 Red flags to watch for * Module 5: Privacy and data handling * 5.1 What NOT to enter * 5.2 Data retention policy * 5.3 Security considerations * Module 6: Practical exercises * Exercise 1: Basic analysis * Exercise 2: Critical review * Exercise 3: Report generation * Training completion checklist * Quick reference card * The golden rule * Key actions * Support contacts * Appendix: Training plan document * Training plan summary * Learning outcomes * Training maintenance Training overview ¶ This training guide provides comprehensive instruction on using the Content Editorial Assistant (CEA), an AI-powered writing analysis tool. Upon completion, users will understand how to effectively use AI assistance while maintaining human oversight and content quality. Training objectives ¶ By the end of this training, you will be able to: 1. Understand the capabilities and limitations of AI-assisted writing tools 2. Apply the human-in-the-loop principle to all AI-generated content 3. Use CEA to analyze and improve technical documentation 4. Identify and mitigate risks associated with AI-generated content 5. Follow best practices for reviewing and accepting AI suggestions Target audience ¶ * Technical writers * Documentation managers * Content reviewers * Anyone using CEA for documentation improvement Estimated training time ¶ * Self-paced learning : 45-60 minutes * Hands-on exercises : 30 minutes * Total : Approximately 1.5 hours Module 1: Understanding AI-assisted writing ¶ 1.1 What is AI-assisted writing? ¶ AI-assisted writing tools use machine learning models to analyze text and provide suggestions for improvement. Unlike traditional spell-checkers, these tools can: * Analyze readability and complexity * Suggest sentence restructuring * Identify ambiguous language * Recommend style improvements * Generate rewritten content 1.2 How CEA uses AI ¶ Content Editorial Assistant employs AI in several ways: Feature How AI is used Human review required Style analysis Rule-based + ML pattern detection Yes - verify suggestions Readability scoring Algorithmic calculations Yes - context matters Ambiguity detection ML-based semantic analysis Yes - may have false positives Content rewriting Large Language Model (LLM) generation Critical - always verify 1.3 AI limitations you must understand ¶ __ | AI systems have inherent limitations: 1. Hallucinations : AI may generate plausible-sounding but incorrect information 2. Context blindness : AI may not understand your product, organization, or domain 3. Bias : AI training data may introduce unintended biases 4. Inconsistency : Same input may produce different outputs 5. No true understanding : AI processes patterns, not meaning ---|--- Module 2: Human-in-the-loop principle ¶ 2.1 What is human-in-the-loop? ¶ Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) is a design principle ensuring humans maintain oversight and final decision authority over AI-generated content. __ ALWAYS REVIEW AI-GENERATED CONTENT PRIOR TO USE This is not optional. Every piece of AI-generated content MUST be reviewed by a human before publication or use. 2.2 Why human review is essential ¶ Risk Human review mitigates Factual errors Humans verify accuracy against source materials Inappropriate tone Humans ensure content matches organizational voice Missing context Humans add domain-specific knowledge Security/Privacy Humans catch sensitive information exposure Brand compliance Humans ensure adherence to style guides 2.3 The review process ¶ Follow this process for ALL AI-generated content: 1. Read completely - Don't skim AI suggestions 2. Verify facts - Cross-reference with authoritative sources 3. Check context - Ensure it fits your document's purpose 4. Validate tone - Confirm appropriate voice and style 5. Test accuracy - For procedures, verify steps work 6. Accept/Modify/Reject - Make deliberate decisions Module 3: Using Content Editorial Assistant ¶ 3.1 Accessing CEA ¶ 1. Navigate to the CEA application URL 2. No login required - sessions are anonymous 3. Your data is NOT stored after the session ends 3.2 Input methods ¶ Uploading documents ¶ Supported formats: * PDF (.pdf) * Microsoft Word (.docx) * Markdown (.md) * AsciiDoc (.adoc) * DITA XML (.dita, .xml) * Plain Text (.txt) Direct text entry ¶ 1. Paste or type content into the text area 2. Useful for quick analysis of snippets 3. No file size limitations for text input 3.3 Running analysis ¶ 1. Click the Analyze button 2. Wait for processing (progress shown in real-time) 3. Review the results dashboard 3.4 Understanding results ¶ Error categories ¶ Category Severity Action required Critical High Must fix before publishing Warning Medium Should address if possible Suggestion Low Optional improvements Readability metrics ¶ * Flesch Reading Ease : Higher = easier (target: 60-70) * Grade level : Target 9 th -11 th grade for technical docs * Gunning Fog Index : Target < 12 for accessibility 3.5 Using AI rewrite feature ¶ __ The rewrite feature generates NEW content using AI. This content requires thorough human review. 1. Locate a content block with issues 2. Click Rewrite button 3. Watch the two-pass refinement: * Pass 1: Initial AI improvement * Pass 2: AI self-refinement 4. REVIEW THE OUTPUT CAREFULLY 5. Choose: Accept / Modify / Reject 3.6 Generating reports ¶ CEA can generate PDF reports of your analysis: * Full report : Comprehensive analysis (8-12 pages) * Executive summary : Key findings only (2-3 pages) __ All PDF reports include the AI disclaimer: "Always review AI-generated content prior to use." Module 4: Best practices ¶ 4.1 Before using AI assistance ¶ Do: * Have a clear goal for your content * Prepare source materials for fact-checking * Understand your audience and purpose Do not: * Enter personal or sensitive information * Rely solely on AI for accuracy 4.2 During AI-assisted work ¶ Do: * Review each suggestion individually * Consider context the AI doesn't have * Use your domain expertise * Document significant AI-suggested changes Do not: * Accept suggestions blindly * Skip the review step 4.3 After using AI assistance ¶ Do: * Perform final human review of all content * Verify technical accuracy * Check for consistency with other documents * Follow your team's review/approval process Do not: * Publish without human verification 4.4 Red flags to watch for ¶ Be especially careful when AI suggestions: * Include specific numbers, dates, or statistics * Reference external sources or links * Make claims about product capabilities * Include code samples or commands * Contradict your source materials Module 5: Privacy and data handling ¶ 5.1 What NOT to enter ¶ __ | Never enter into CEA: * Personal Identifiable Information (PII) * Customer data or account information * Credentials, passwords, or API keys * Confidential business information * Information not intended for AI processing ---|--- 5.2 Data retention policy ¶ * Session data : Deleted when you close the browser * Analysis results : Not stored after session * Uploaded documents : Processed in-memory only * AI interactions : Not used for model training 5.3 Security considerations ¶ * CEA runs within the corporate network * Content is processed by approved AI providers only * No data is shared with external parties * Session isolation prevents data leakage Module 6: Practical exercises ¶ Exercise 1: Basic analysis ¶ 1. Copy a paragraph from existing documentation 2. Paste into CEA text input 3. Click Analyze 4. Review results and identify: * How many issues were found ? * What is the readability score ? * What ' s the most common issue type ? Exercise 2: Critical review ¶ 1. Use the rewrite feature on a content block 2. Before accepting, answer: * Is the rewritten content factually accurate? * Does it maintain the original meaning? * Is the tone appropriate? * Would you publish this as-is? Exercise 3: Report generation ¶ 1. Run analysis on a document 2. Generate both Full Report and Executive Summary 3. Note the AI disclaimer on each page 4. Identify three actionable items from the report Training completion checklist ¶ Before considering this training complete, verify you can answer YES to all: * I understand that AI can generate incorrect information (hallucinations) * I will ALWAYS review AI-generated content before use * I know NOT to enter personal or sensitive information * I understand CEA's analysis features and their limitations * I can interpret readability scores and error categories * I know how to use the rewrite feature responsibly * I understand data is not retained after my session * I know where to report issues or provide feedback Quick reference card ¶ The golden rule ¶ __ ALWAYS REVIEW AI-GENERATED CONTENT PRIOR TO USE Key actions ¶ * Analyze : Process content for issues * Rewrite : Generate AI improvements (requires review!) * Full report : Comprehensive PDF analysis * Summary : Executive-level PDF overview Support contacts ¶ * Slack : #content-editorial-assistant * Email : gtrivedi@redhat.com * Issues : GitLab Issues Appendix: Training plan document ¶ Training plan summary ¶ Item Details Training title Content Editorial Assistant AI-assisted writing training Version 1.0 Effective date November 2025 Target audience All CEA users (technical writers, documentation teams) Delivery method Self-paced online training via Antora documentation Duration 1.5 hours (including exercises) Prerequisites Basic technical writing experience Assessment Completion checklist (self-assessment) Learning outcomes ¶ Upon completion, learners will demonstrate: 1. Knowledge of AI capabilities and limitations 2. Understanding of human-in-the-loop requirements 3. Ability to use CEA features effectively 4. Competence in reviewing AI-generated content 5. Awareness of privacy and data handling requirements Training maintenance ¶ This training guide will be reviewed and updated: * Quarterly for accuracy * When significant features are added * When AI policies change * Based on user feedback Last updated 2025-11-25 17:03:30 +0530

docs.title: 4.3 After using AI assistance

docs.text: Be especially careful when AI suggestions: * Include specific numbers, dates, or statistics * Reference external sources or links * Make claims about product capabilities * Include code samples or commands * Contradict your source materials
docs.title: 4.4 Red flags to watch for

docs.text: __ | Never enter into CEA: * Personal Identifiable Information (PII) * Customer data or account information * Credentials, passwords, or API keys * Confidential business information * Information not intended for AI processing ---|---
docs.title: 5.1 What NOT to enter

docs.text: * Session data : Deleted when you close the browser * Analysis results : Not stored after session * Uploaded documents : Processed in-memory only * AI interactions : Not used for model training
docs.title: 5.2 Data retention policy

docs.text: * CEA runs within the corporate network * Content is processed by approved AI providers only * No data is shared with external parties * Session isolation prevents data leakage
docs.title: 5.3 Security considerations

docs.text: 1. Copy a paragraph from existing documentation 2. Paste into CEA text input 3. Click Analyze 4. Review results and identify: * How many issues were found ? * What is the readability score ? * What ' s the most common issue type ?
docs.title: Exercise 1: Basic analysis

docs.text: 1. Use the rewrite feature on a content block 2. Before accepting, answer: * Is the rewritten content factually accurate? * Does it maintain the original meaning? * Is the tone appropriate? * Would you publish this as-is?
docs.title: Exercise 2: Critical review

docs.text: 1. Run analysis on a document 2. Generate both Full Report and Executive Summary 3. Note the AI disclaimer on each page 4. Identify three actionable items from the report
docs.title: Exercise 3: Report generation

docs.text: Before considering this training complete, verify you can answer YES to all: * I understand that AI can generate incorrect information (hallucinations) * I will ALWAYS review AI-generated content before use * I know NOT to enter personal or sensitive information * I understand CEA's analysis features and their limitations * I can interpret readability scores and error categories * I know how to use the rewrite feature responsibly * I understand data is not retained after my session * I know where to report issues or provide feedback
docs.title: Training completion checklist

docs.text: __ ALWAYS REVIEW AI-GENERATED CONTENT PRIOR TO USE
docs.title: The golden rule

docs.text: * Analyze : Process content for issues * Rewrite : Generate AI improvements (requires review!) * Full report : Comprehensive PDF analysis * Summary : Executive-level PDF overview

docs.text: Item Details Training title Content Editorial Assistant AI-assisted writing training Version 1.0 Effective date November 2025 Target audience All CEA users (technical writers, documentation teams) Delivery method Self-paced online training via Antora documentation Duration 1.5 hours (including exercises) Prerequisites Basic technical writing experience Assessment Completion checklist (self-assessment)
docs.title: Training plan summary

docs.text: Upon completion, learners will demonstrate: 1. Knowledge of AI capabilities and limitations 2. Understanding of human-in-the-loop requirements 3. Ability to use CEA features effectively 4. Competence in reviewing AI-generated content 5. Awareness of privacy and data handling requirements

docs.text: Writing Tests ¶ Learn how to write effective tests for the Content Editorial Assistant. Test Organization ¶ [code] tests/ ├── unit/ # Individual component tests │ ├── test_rewriter.py │ ├── test_style_analyzer.py │ ├── rules/ # Rule-specific tests │ └── test_ .py │ ├── integration/ # Multi-component tests │ ├── test_metadata_api.py │ ├── test_block_rewrite.py │ └── test_ .py │ ├── api/ # API endpoint tests ├── database/ # Database operation tests ├── frontend/ # Frontend component tests ├── ui/ # Browser automation tests ├── websocket/ # WebSocket tests ├── validation/ # Validation system tests └── performance/ # Speed benchmarks [/code] Unit Test Examples ¶ Basic Unit Test ¶ [code] import pytest from mymodule import MyFeature @pytest . mark . unit def test_my_feature () : """Test that MyFeature works correctly.""" feature = MyFeature () result = feature . process ( "input" ) assert result == "expected_output" [/code] Testing with Fixtures ¶ [code] @pytest.fixture def sample_text(): """Provide sample text for testing.""" return "This is a sample sentence for testing." @pytest . mark . unit def test_style_analysis ( sample_text ): """Test style analyzer with fixture.""" from style_analyzer import StyleAnalyzer analyzer = StyleAnalyzer () result = analyzer . analyze ( sample_text ) assert 'readability_score' in result assert result [ 'readability_score' ] > 0 [/code] Testing Exceptions ¶ [code] @pytest.mark.unit def test_invalid_input_raises_error(): """Test that invalid input raises appropriate error.""" from mymodule import process_document with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="Invalid document format"): process_document(None) [/code] Integration Test Examples ¶ API Integration Test ¶ [code] @pytest.mark.integration def test_complete_workflow(client, db): """Test end-to-end analysis workflow.""" # Upload document with open('test_doc.txt', 'rb') as f: response = client.post('/upload', data={'file': f}, content_type='multipart/form-data') assert response.status_code == 200 doc_id = response.json['document_id'] # Analyze document response = client.post(f'/analyze/{doc_id}') assert response.status_code == 200 assert 'errors' in response.json assert 'suggestions' in response.json [/code] Database Integration Test ¶ [code] @pytest.mark.database def test_database_transaction(db_service): """Test database transaction handling.""" # Create record record_id = db_service.create_feedback({ 'content': 'Test feedback', 'rating': 5 }) # Verify created record = db_service.get_feedback(record_id) assert record is not None assert record['rating'] == 5 # Update record db_service.update_feedback(record_id, {'rating': 4}) updated = db_service.get_feedback(record_id) assert updated['rating'] == 4 [/code] UI Test Examples ¶ Browser Automation with Playwright ¶ [code] @pytest.mark.ui def test_ui_interaction(page): """Test user interface with Playwright.""" # Navigate to app page.goto(" http://localhost:5000 ") # Enter text page.fill("textarea#content", "Sample text to analyze") # Click analyze button page.click("button#analyze") # Wait for results page.wait_for_selector(".results", timeout=5000) # Verify results displayed assert page.is_visible(".results") assert page.is_visible(".error-list") [/code] Testing Form Submission ¶ [code] @pytest.mark.ui def test_form_submission(page): """Test form submission and validation.""" page.goto(" http://localhost:5000 ") # Fill form page . fill ( "input#email" , "test@example.com" ) page . fill ( "textarea#content" , "Test content" ) # Submit page . click ( "button[type='submit']" ) # Check success message success = page . locator ( ".alert-success" ) expect ( success ). to_be_visible () [/code] Performance Test Examples ¶ Benchmark Test ¶ [code] @pytest.mark.performance def test_rewriter_performance(benchmark): """Benchmark rewriter performance.""" from rewriter import DocumentRewriter rewriter = DocumentRewriter() sample_text = "This is a test sentence. " * 100 # Run benchmark result = benchmark(rewriter.rewrite, sample_text) # Verify it completed assert result is not None assert len(result) > 0 [/code] Load Test ¶ [code] @pytest.mark.performance def test_concurrent_requests(): """Test system under concurrent load.""" import concurrent.futures import requests def make_request(): response = requests.post('http://localhost:5000/analyze', json={'text': 'Test content'}) return response.status_code == 200 # Run 50 concurrent requests with concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=10) as executor: futures = [executor.submit(make_request) for _ in range(50)] results = [f.result() for f in futures] # At least 95% should succeed success_rate = sum(results) / len(results) assert success_rate >= 0.95 [/code] WebSocket Test Examples ¶ [code] @pytest.mark.websocket def test_websocket_connection(websocket_client): """Test WebSocket real-time communication.""" # Connect ws = websocket_client('/ws/progress') # Send message ws . send_json ({ ' action ' : ' start_analysis ' , ' doc_id ' : ' 123 ' }) # Receive progress updates message = ws . receive_json ( timeout = 5 ) assert message [ ' type ' ] == ' progress ' assert ' percent ' in message # Close connection ws . close () [/code] Best Practices ¶ DO ✅ ¶ * Write tests for new features immediately [code] # Good: Test written alongside feature def new_feature(): pass def test_new_feature(): result = new_feature() assert result is not None [/code] * Keep tests independent [code] # Good: Each test is independent def test_feature_a(): result_a = feature_a() assert result_a == "expected" def test_feature_b(): result_b = feature_b() assert result_b == "expected" [/code] * Use clear, descriptive names [code] # Good: Name describes what is being tested def test_rewriter_handles_long_sentences(): pass def test_style_analyzer_detects_passive_voice(): pass [/code] * Mock external dependencies [code] # Good: Mock external API @patch('requests.get') def test_api_call(mock_get): mock_get.return_value.json.return_value = {'data': 'test'} result = fetch_data() assert result == {'data': 'test'} [/code] DON'T ❌ ¶ * Skip writing tests "for now" [code] # Bad: No test for new feature def new_feature(): pass # TODO: Write tests later (never happens) [/code] * Have tests depend on each other [code] # Bad: test_b depends on test_a running first def test_a(): global shared_data shared_data = create_data() def test_b(): use_data(shared_data) # Breaks if test_a doesn't run first [/code] * Use vague names [code] # Bad: Unclear what is being tested def test_1(): pass def test_something(): pass [/code] * Test external APIs directly [code] # Bad: Direct call to external API def test_weather(): response = requests.get(' https://api.weather.com/ ...') # Flaky! assert response.status_code == 200 [/code] Common Patterns ¶ Parametrized Tests ¶ Test multiple inputs efficiently: [code] @pytest.mark.parametrize("input,expected", [ ("short", True), ("This is a very long sentence that exceeds limits", False), ("", True), ]) def test_sentence_length(input, expected): """Test sentence length validation.""" result = is_sentence_acceptable(input) assert result == expected [/code] Fixture Factories ¶ Create reusable test data: [code] @pytest.fixture def make_document(): """Factory for creating test documents.""" def _make(title="Test", content="Content"): return { 'title': title, 'content': content, 'created': datetime.now() } return _make def test_with_factory(make_document): """Use fixture factory.""" doc1 = make_document(title="Doc 1") doc2 = make_document(title="Doc 2", content="Different content") assert doc1['title'] != doc2['title'] [/code] Temporary Files ¶ [code] import tempfile from pathlib import Path def test_file_processing(): """Test with temporary file.""" with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as tmpdir: test_file = Path(tmpdir) / "test.txt" test_file.write_text("Test content") result = process_file(test_file) assert result is not None # tmpdir automatically cleaned up [/code] Running Your Tests ¶ [code] # Run specific test pytest tests/unit/test_rewriter.py::test_my_feature -v # Run all tests in a file pytest tests/unit/test_rewriter.py -v # Run tests matching a pattern pytest -k "rewriter" -v # Run with coverage pytest tests/unit/ --cov=rewriter --cov-report=html # Run in parallel pytest tests/ -n auto [/code] Next Steps ¶ * Learn about AI-powered analysis * Set up testing infrastructure * Return to documentation home Resources ¶ * Pytest Documentation * Playwright Documentation * Shared Test Fixtures Last updated 2025-11-24 22:49:07 +0530
